proposals.title: Probabilistic Mapping of Liquefaction Severity via Multi-Sensor Data Fusion and Ensemble Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction susceptibility maps are often binary, low-resolution, and lack uncertainty quantification, limiting their utility for performance-based seismic risk assessment of infrastructure. The 2010-2011 Canterbury earthquake sequence demonstrated the severe spatial variability of liquefaction severity, which existing regional maps failed to capture. A high-resolution, probabilistic forecasting tool is urgently needed to inform land-use planning, retrofit prioritization, and loss estimation models.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model trained on spatially co-located, multi-source geospatial data (InSAR, CPT/ERT, LiDAR, seismic intensity) can predict continuous liquefaction severity indices with quantified spatial uncertainty, significantly outperforming traditional index-based binary models.
proposals.method: We will develop a data fusion framework using a stacked ensemble model (e.g., Random Forest and Gradient Boosting base learners, with a Gaussian Process meta-learner). Input features will include time-series InSAR coherence loss (precursors), 3D soil resistance from CPT/ERT logs, LiDAR-derived topographic wetness index (proxy for water table), and scenario-based PGA. The model will be trained and validated on high-quality case studies (e.g., Canterbury, NZ; 2011 Tohoku). The Gaussian Process layer will provide probabilistic predictions and uncertainty maps.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, open-source framework for generating high-resolution probabilistic liquefaction severity maps, directly feeding into performance-based assessment tools like OpenSRA. It will advance the field from binary susceptibility towards a continuous, uncertainty-aware severity metric, improving risk assessments for slopes and distributed infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally adapts the data fusion paradigm from precision agriculture to geotechnical engineering. It is novel in its explicit fusion of InSAR time-series as a precursor signal with 3D subsurface data for liquefaction prediction and its use of ensemble ML to output full probability distributions of severity.

proposals.title: A Microstructural Investigation of Liquefaction Susceptibility in Heterogeneous Shallow Soils Using X-ray Micro-Computed Tomography
proposals.motivation: Empirical liquefaction triggering procedures (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) rely heavily on simplified metrics like SPT or CPT resistance and deposit-scale uniformity, potentially underestimating risk in shallow, heterogeneous, or native soils. High-profile failures in such strata, like those observed in the 2010-2011 Canterbury earthquake sequence, suggest localized microscale soil architecture may control liquefaction manifestation. A physics-based, microstructural understanding is needed to move beyond these empirical generalizations and improve performance-based assessment frameworks like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: The liquefaction susceptibility of shallow, heterogeneous soils is governed not by average index properties, but by specific, quantifiable microscale configurations—such as localized loose pockets, abrupt permeability boundaries, and anisotropic fabric—that create critical internal weaknesses susceptible to undrained instability.
proposals.method: We will collect undisturbed block samples of shallow, heterogeneous soils from sites conventionally deemed low-liquefaction risk. Samples will be scanned using X-ray micro-CT (e.g., a Zeiss Xradia 520 Versa) at resolutions down to 5-10 microns to generate 3D digital soil models. Image processing (using Avizo or ImageJ/Fiji) will segment particles, pores, and contacts. Quantitative analysis will extract metrics of pore network connectivity, particle orientation fabric via tensor analysis, and spatial distribution of local void ratio. These metrics will be correlated with cyclic direct simple shear test results on companion specimens to identify critical microstructural signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, 3D microstructural catalog linking soil fabric to liquefaction resistance in heterogeneous shallow deposits. It will provide a mechanistic basis for challenging empirical 'uniform deposit' assumptions and inform the development of next-generation, microstructure-informed constitutive models for performance-based seismic assessment of slopes and infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While micro-CT has been used for idealized sands, its systematic application to native, heterogeneous field soils to decouple liquefaction from deposit uniformity is novel. This bridges the gap between geotechnical earthquake engineering and soil micromorphology, targeting a critical blind spot in current hazard models.

proposals.title: A Coupled Network-Continuum Framework for Simulating Seismically-Induced Cascading Infrastructure Failures via Soil-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: Current seismic risk assessments for infrastructure systems, such as those in OpenSRA, often treat soil as a static boundary condition or use simplified interaction rules, neglecting the dynamic, physics-based feedback between failing structures and the surrounding soil continuum. This gap limits the ability to predict cascading failures where the settlement or liquefaction-induced loss of support for one structure alters the stress state and bearing capacity for adjacent foundations. A mechanistic understanding of this soil-mediated failure propagation is critical for the resilience of dense urban networks and distributed lifelines.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly coupling a network model of infrastructure connectivity with a high-resolution, effective-stress soil continuum model will enable the physically realistic simulation of cascading failures, where the primary propagation mechanism is the dynamic redistribution of stresses and pore pressures in the shared soil medium following initial component damage.
proposals.method: The framework will couple a graph-based network model (nodes: structures/lifelines; edges: functional dependencies) with a finite element continuum model using the OpenSees platform for simulating soil under cyclic loading. The network will be updated based on performance limits (e.g., excessive settlement from the continuum model), while structural demands from the network will impose new boundary conditions on the soil mesh. Key algorithms include a staggered explicit-implicit coupling scheme and adaptive remeshing around zones of progressive soil failure (e.g., shear banding or liquefaction). The continuum model will employ a critical state soil plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand) to capture pore pressure generation and large deformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool that moves beyond abstract topological failure propagation to model the actual geomechanical drivers of infrastructure interdependence. It will provide a validated methodology for simulating soil-mediated cascades, directly enhancing performance-based seismic assessment of slope and foundation systems. The resulting framework will offer a more physically rigorous basis for risk mitigation strategies in liquefaction-prone regions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the bidirectional, mechanistic coupling where the soil continuum is not a passive layer but an active medium for failure transmission, integrating network theory with advanced geotechnical effective-stress analysis in a single simulation environment. This moves beyond current practice of sequential or uncoupled analyses.

proposals.title: A Network-Theoretic Framework for Infrastructure Resilience Using High-Resolution X-ray CT Soil Tomography
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessments of distributed infrastructure, like natural gas networks, rely on simplified soil models and abstract network connectivity, which fail to capture the complex, three-dimensional soil heterogeneity that controls localized liquefaction and differential ground deformation. This limitation hinders accurate prediction of cascading failures, such as pipe breaks propagating through liquefied zones, posing a significant risk to community resilience. High-resolution imaging of actual soil fabric and pore structure offers a pathway to ground network models in physical reality.
proposals.hypothesis: That constructing a physics-based interaction network of infrastructure elements, where connection strengths are derived from constitutive soil properties mapped via X-ray Computed Tomography (CT), will enable mechanistic simulation of seismic failure propagation (e.g., from liquefaction-induced settlement) with significantly greater fidelity than current empirical or homogenized soil models.
proposals.method: The research will first acquire high-resolution 3D soil models using laboratory-based X-ray CT scanning of undisturbed samples from critical sites. Image segmentation algorithms (e.g., watershed, machine learning-based) will quantify pore structure, particle contacts, and material interfaces. These spatial maps will inform spatially variable constitutive model parameters (e.g., for PM4Sand or PM4Silt) within a finite element framework. Infrastructure nodes (e.g., pipe joints, foundation elements) will be connected through this soil mesh, with interaction strengths defined by the imaged soil's shear resistance and stiffness. Network analysis and graph theory will then be applied to simulate cascading failures under seismic loading, validated against centrifuge test data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source simulation framework that fuses geotechnical imaging with network science, directly contributing to tools like OpenSRA. It will advance the state of performance-based assessment by providing a validated method to quantify systemic risk from soil-structure interaction in spatially complex deposits, moving beyond the 'perfectly connected' network assumption.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of medical-grade 3D soil tomography with physics-based network theory to simulate infrastructure failure propagation is a radical departure from current practice, which either uses abstract networks or continuum models without explicit, high-resolution soil fabric data to define inter-nodal connections.

proposals.title: Inversion of Spatially-Distributed Ground Motions via Remote Sensing of Liquefaction-Induced Deformation
proposals.motivation: Rapid and accurate characterization of strong ground shaking following major earthquakes is critical for emergency response and loss assessment, yet dense instrumental networks are often unavailable. Liquefaction-induced ground deformation, such as settlement and lateral spreading, is a widespread manifestation of shaking that can be mapped rapidly over large areas using satellite InSAR and airborne LiDAR. This deformation provides a spatially continuous, physically-based constraint on the shaking that caused it, offering a novel data source for seismic analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial pattern and magnitude of liquefaction-induced ground deformation, derived from remote sensing, can be used within an inverse analysis framework to estimate key ground motion parameters, such as peak ground velocity and significant duration, across the affected region, thereby refining shaking maps where instrumental data is sparse.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inverse analysis framework that links predicted liquefaction-induced deformation to causative ground motions. The forward model will integrate established empirical liquefaction triggering models (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss) with effective stress-based deformation models for settlement and lateral spreading. Pre- and post-event InSAR (Sentinel-1) and LiDAR data will provide the observational constraint of deformation. The inversion algorithm will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate the posterior distribution of shaking parameters at grid points, constrained by the remote sensing data and prior information from regional ground motion models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for generating high-resolution, observationally-constrained shaking maps after major earthquakes, independent of dense seismic networks. It will advance the field of performance-based seismic assessment by directly linking system performance (liquefaction deformation) to input motions. The refined shaking estimates will directly improve regional loss assessment tools like OpenSRA and inform urgent post-event response.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing is used to *map* liquefaction effects, its direct use as a quantitative constraint for *inverting* the causative ground motion parameters is a novel synthesis of geotechnical engineering and seismology. This approach creates a new source of shaking data from the damage itself, bypassing instrumental limitations.

proposals.title: A Kinetic Model for Cumulative Soil Fabric Degradation: Adapting Polymer Chain Scission Theory for Sequential Earthquake Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of liquefiable soils, including those in OpenSRA, often relies on models calibrated for single seismic events. This is insufficient for critical infrastructure facing sequential earthquakes, as observed in mainshock-aftershock sequences, where damage accumulates. A constitutive framework that explicitly tracks the progressive, irreversible degradation of soil fabric across multiple loading cycles is needed to improve risk estimates for natural gas pipelines and slope systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The cumulative breakdown of load-bearing inter-particle contacts in saturated granular soil under cyclic loading can be mathematically described by adapting kinetic models for polymer chain scission, providing a fundamental state variable for predicting accelerated strength loss in subsequent seismic events.
proposals.method: We will adapt the kinetic equations for polymer chain scission (e.g., the Kuhn model) to formulate a 'fabric scission' rate law, where the rate of contact breakage is a function of current fabric state and cyclic stress intensity. This will be implemented as a new internal state variable within an effective stress, critical state-based constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand framework). The model will be calibrated and validated against high-quality cyclic direct simple shear and centrifuge test data involving multi-stage loading sequences, simulating mainshock-aftershock events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel constitutive modeling framework that explicitly quantifies cumulative fabric damage, moving beyond cycle-counting with a physics-inspired analogy. It will directly enhance the predictive capability of performance-based seismic assessment tools like OpenSRA for multi-event scenarios, leading to more accurate estimates of post-liquefaction deformations and sediment ejecta severity.
proposals.novelty: While damage accumulation is recognized, this is the first proposed application of polymer degradation kinetics to model soil fabric evolution. The approach provides a rigorous, mechanistic alternative to empirical cycle-based degradation rules, formalizing the concept of sequential fabric 'scission'.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Sequential Liquefaction: Replicating the 2010-2011 Canterbury Earthquake Sequence on Christchurch Soil Analogs
proposals.motivation: The 2010-2011 Canterbury earthquake sequence caused unprecedented and progressive liquefaction damage in Christchurch, challenging conventional seismic assessment methods that treat mainshocks in isolation. Pre-2026 practice lacks validated physical models to quantify how prior shaking alters soil state and resistance, thereby influencing the performance of slopes and foundations in subsequent events. This gap limits the accuracy of performance-based assessments for infrastructure in seismically active regions.
proposals.hypothesis: The severity of liquefaction and associated ground failure (e.g., sliding, ejecta) during a mainshock is critically dependent on the pre-shearing and pore pressure redistribution imparted by foreshocks, which can be systematically replicated and measured in centrifuge models. We hypothesize that sequential shaking leads to a non-linear accumulation of damage not predicted by single-event analyses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of centrifuge tests on reconstituted specimens of Christchurch soil analogs (e.g., sandy silt to silty sand). Using a servo-hydraulic shaker, we will apply scaled acceleration time histories representing the Darfield (2010) foreshock and Christchurch (2011) mainshock sequences. Key measurements include pore pressure generation, shear wave velocity (via bender elements), and soil deformation. The tests will be analyzed within an effective stress framework, with results used to calibrate and validate numerical models in OpenSRA or similar platforms, focusing on the evolving state parameter and cyclic resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-quality, repeatable physical evidence of how sequential earthquakes modify liquefaction triggering and severity. It will deliver calibrated soil parameters for sequential loading, directly improving the predictive capability of performance-based tools like OpenSRA for assessing slope systems and buried infrastructure. The dataset will serve as a benchmark for validating advanced constitutive models.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge modeling of single liquefaction events is common, the controlled physical simulation of a documented, multi-event seismic sequence with precise attention to the evolving in-situ state is novel. This bridges the gap between field case histories and laboratory element tests, explicitly testing the 'memory' of soil between real-world earthquakes.

proposals.title: A Multi-Fidelity Probabilistic Framework for Liquefaction Forecasting: Bridging Regional Hazard Maps to Site-Specific Digital Twins
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessment faces a critical resolution gap. Regional models, while useful for screening, lack the site-specific detail needed for engineering design and critical infrastructure risk management. Conversely, developing high-fidelity models for every site is prohibitively expensive. A systematic framework to guide resource allocation from regional screening to detailed site analysis is urgently needed for resilient infrastructure planning.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid forecasting system that uses a regional probabilistic model to guide and initialize high-resolution, physics-based digital twins for prioritized sites will provide more accurate and actionable forecasts of liquefaction severity and its consequences than either approach used in isolation.
proposals.method: The framework will first implement a regional probabilistic model using established CPT-based liquefaction triggering procedures (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) within a Bayesian updating scheme. This model will identify 'critical zones.' For these zones, site-specific digital twins will be constructed using the OpenSees computational platform, employing PM4Sand and PM4Silt constitutive models for effective stress analysis of liquefaction response. The digital twins will be probabilistically initialized using the regional model's outputs as priors and will assimilate local data (e.g., CPT, Vs, groundwater) through ensemble Kalman filtering to reduce forecast uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, operational multi-fidelity framework for liquefaction risk assessment, directly linking regional hazard mapping to detailed performance-based engineering analysis. It will provide a methodology for rationally prioritizing subsurface investigation resources and will produce a library of calibrated, probabilistic digital twins for high-consequence sites, improving the reliability of risk estimates for infrastructure like natural gas networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal, probabilistic coupling of regional screening models with site-specific digital twins, creating a continuously updatable system that bridges geospatial hazard analysis and high-resolution geotechnical simulation, an integration not yet fully realized in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale, Adaptive Mesh Framework for Probabilistic Regional Liquefaction Hazard Forecasting
proposals.motivation: Current regional-scale liquefaction hazard models face a critical trade-off between computational tractability and spatial resolution. High-fidelity site-specific analyses are too expensive for city-wide or portfolio-level risk assessments, while coarse models lack the detail needed for critical infrastructure planning. This gap hinders the efficient deployment of resources for seismic risk mitigation, as seen in tools like OpenSRA which require scalable solutions for large geographical areas.
proposals.hypothesis: Adaptive mesh refinement (AMR), a technique proven in computational fluid dynamics, can be adapted to create a dual-scale probabilistic liquefaction forecasting system that dynamically allocates computational resources to high-probability or high-uncertainty zones, thereby achieving both regional coverage and local accuracy efficiently.
proposals.method: The method will develop a probabilistic framework where a coarse-scale model, informed by regional geology, hydrology, and seismic hazard, computes an initial liquefaction triggering probability field. This field will guide an AMR algorithm (e.g., based on error estimation or probability gradients) to iteratively refine the spatial grid in targeted zones. Refined cells will employ higher-resolution geotechnical models (e.g., CPT-based liquefaction triggering procedures coupled with performance-based sliding block analyses) to estimate localized severity and displacement. The computational architecture will be tested using pre-2026 case studies of liquefiable sedimentary basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient methodology for regional liquefaction assessment that explicitly links coarse-scale hazard with site-specific performance. It will provide a formalized, automated process for prioritizing high-resolution analysis, directly enhancing the capabilities of performance-based seismic risk platforms for infrastructure systems.
proposals.novelty: While AMR is standard in CFD, its application to geotechnical earthquake engineering for intelligent, probability-driven model refinement is novel. This approach moves beyond static multi-scale models by creating a dynamic, automated system that adapts its resolution based on the evolving hazard forecast.

proposals.title: A Real-Time Data Assimilation Framework for Dynamic Probabilistic Liquefaction Severity Forecasting During Earthquake Sequences
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessments are static, based on pre-event geotechnical models and scenario shaking. This fails to capture the dynamic evolution of ground failure risk during an actual seismic sequence, where real-time shaking intensity, spatial variability, and pore-pressure response critically influence outcomes. Emergency managers and infrastructure operators, such as those overseeing natural gas networks via tools like OpenSRA, need live, high-resolution forecasts to prioritize post-event inspections and mitigative actions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time seismic and geotechnical sensor data (ground motion, pore pressure, water table) into a probabilistic liquefaction triggering and severity framework will enable accurate, dynamically updated forecasts of liquefaction manifestation (e.g., severity of sediment ejecta) at a city-block scale as an earthquake unfolds.
proposals.method: The framework will fuse a regional-scale probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) model with a high-resolution geospatial engine. It will employ an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to assimilate real-time data streams from seismic networks and instrumented geotechnical arrays. The downscaling will use pre-compiled geospatial layers of CPT-based soil resistance and state, updated with assimilated pore-pressure data, within a performance-based effective stress analysis framework to compute probabilistic estimates of liquefaction severity indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first operational prototype for dynamic liquefaction forecasting, moving the field from static hazard mapping to live risk assessment. It will directly enhance performance-based seismic assessment of distributed infrastructure by providing time-evolving ground failure inputs. The open-source software will extend the capabilities of platforms like OpenSRA for real-time risk management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time data assimilation and dynamic downscaling for liquefaction forecasting, a significant advance over pre-computed scenario-based maps. It uniquely integrates evolving pore-pressure observations directly into a regional probabilistic framework to update severity forecasts at high resolution during an event.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Designing Self-Adapting Geomaterials to Mitigate Liquefaction in Seismic Sequences
proposals.motivation: Earthquake sequences and aftershocks pose a significant, under-addressed risk to infrastructure on liquefiable soils, as the initial event can weaken the ground, increasing vulnerability to subsequent shaking. Current soil improvement techniques are static and cannot adapt to evolving seismic demands. This research addresses the critical need for soils that can autonomously increase their resistance during an earthquake sequence, thereby protecting critical infrastructure like natural gas networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Geomaterials engineered with dormant chemical or biological agents can be activated by the mechanical agitation and pore pressure changes of initial strong shaking, triggering rapid beneficial changes that increase the soil's cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) before subsequent seismic events.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled experimental and numerical framework. First, candidate agents (e.g., microbially induced carbonate precipitation (MICP) precursors, colloidal silica, or low-viscosity polymers) will be mixed into sand specimens. These will be subjected to staged cyclic simple shear tests, simulating an initial mainshock, a pause, and a subsequent aftershock, while monitoring shear wave velocity and pore pressure. The performance-based assessment methodology from OpenSRA will be adapted to model the time-evolving state of the soil, using effective stress analysis to quantify changes in post-mainshock liquefaction severity and sliding potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a new class of 'autonomous' geomaterial with quantified performance gains for seismic sequences, 2) a novel performance-based assessment framework that accounts for time-variant soil properties during shaking, and 3) validated constitutive model parameters for effective stress analysis of self-adapting soils.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static soil improvement by designing a material whose post-shaking state is intentionally more resilient than its initial state, introducing the concept of real-time, triggered adaptation within the soil matrix itself during an earthquake sequence.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Designing Multi-Stage, Self-Modifying Soils to Mitigate Sequential Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Earthquake sequences, including foreshocks and aftershocks, present a significant challenge to geotechnical infrastructure as soils weakened by an initial event are more vulnerable to subsequent liquefaction. Current design practices, often based on single-event scenarios, may underestimate this cumulative damage. There is a critical need for advanced soil design strategies that can adaptively respond to sequential seismic loading to improve resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering composite soils with inclusions designed to activate at distinct cyclic shear strain thresholds, the soil's state (e.g., pore fluid viscosity, dilatancy) can be beneficially modified in stages, thereby preserving shear resistance and reducing liquefaction severity during a sequence of seismic events.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled experimental and numerical performance-based framework. We will design and fabricate soil composites incorporating sequential-response elements, such as crushable silica shells filled with pore-fluid viscosifiers (e.g., silica gel precursors) and biodegradable polymer fibers with staged tensile failure points. A program of cyclic direct simple shear and centrifuge tests will subject these composites to realistic seismic sequences. The experimental data will calibrate and validate effective stress-based numerical models in OpenSees or similar platforms, extending the performance-based assessment methods from Dr. Bray's prior work on slopes and sediment ejecta to quantify the multi-stage system performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, bio-inspired design paradigm for engineered soils, moving beyond passive analysis to active, staged defense. It will produce a new performance-based assessment methodology for sequential liquefaction and generate prototype soil composite designs with quantifiable performance metrics for critical infrastructure protection.
proposals.novelty: The approach is fundamentally novel by translating the concept of triggered, sequential release from biomedical drug delivery to geotechnical materials engineering, creating soils with an intrinsic, multi-phase defensive 'metabolism' against seismic sequences, which is not addressed by conventional ground improvement techniques.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Quantifying Site-Specific PGV Amplification from Strong Rock Motions Using Effective Stress Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current ground motion models (GMMs) for peak ground velocity (PGV) often rely on simplified site proxies like Vs30, which may inadequately capture nonlinear site response and liquefaction effects during intense shaking. This gap leads to significant uncertainty in seismic hazard assessments for critical infrastructure, particularly for Bray's focus areas like natural gas pipelines and slopes. A mechanistic, site-specific model for PGV amplification is urgently needed to improve performance-based seismic design and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Site-specific PGV amplification for intense rock motions is primarily controlled by the coupled effects of soil nonlinearity, pore pressure generation, and the evolving state of stress, which can be robustly predicted using effective stress analysis calibrated against high-quality case histories.
proposals.method: The research will leverage and extend the OpenSRA platform to implement a modular, effective stress analysis (ESA) framework for 1D site response. The core method involves: (1) compiling a database of recorded strong motions (rock and site) with detailed geotechnical site characterization; (2) implementing a constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand, UBC3D-PLM) within a nonlinear ESA solver to simulate pore pressure rise and stiffness degradation; (3) developing a machine learning surrogate model (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) trained on ESA results to predict site-specific amplification factors (AF_PGV) as a function of input rock PGV, soil state, and stratigraphy; and (4) validating the model against case histories of liquefaction-induced ground failure and sediment ejecta.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source module for OpenSRA that quantifies PGV amplification for performance-based assessment of liquefiable sites. It will provide a direct link between advanced geotechnical analysis (ESA) and a key ground motion intensity measure (PGV) used in infrastructure vulnerability models. The resulting framework will reduce epistemic uncertainty in seismic risk analysis for slopes and buried infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While ESA is used for liquefaction triggering, its direct application to predict site-specific PGV amplification factors for intense shaking, and the development of a practical surrogate model for engineering risk platforms, represents a significant advance beyond conventional equivalent-linear or Vs30-based approaches.

proposals.title: Performance-Based Assessment of Low-Magnitude Velocity Pulse Damage to Stiff Urban Structures on Cohesive Soils
proposals.motivation: Current seismic hazard assessments and building codes primarily focus on damage from large-magnitude events, often overlooking the high-frequency occurrence of smaller earthquakes (M4-6). Recent observations, such as from the 2014 South Napa earthquake, indicate that even moderate events can generate near-source, short-period velocity pulses capable of causing significant damage to stiff, low-rise structures common in urban environments (e.g., shear-wall buildings, parking garages). A systematic, performance-based framework to quantify this risk is lacking, leading to potential underestimation of cumulative damage and lifecycle costs for critical urban infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inelastic demand on stiff, short-period structures from low-to-moderate magnitude earthquakes with distinct velocity pulses is significantly underestimated by conventional ground motion models and elastic design spectra, particularly when site-specific soil-structure interaction on cohesive, non-liquefiable ground is considered.
proposals.method: The research will employ a performance-based seismic assessment framework, extending the researcher's prior work on slopes and liquefaction. We will utilize the OpenSRA platform to develop a new module. The method involves: (1) curating a database of recorded ground motions from M4-6 earthquakes containing identified velocity pulses; (2) conducting effective stress-based nonlinear site response analyses for a range of stiff, cohesive soil profiles to generate structure-specific input motions; (3) modeling representative stiff structural systems using nonlinear finite element analysis with a focus on shear-dominated failure mechanisms; and (4) implementing a sliding surface analysis to assess the transient instability potential of structural elements under pulse-type excitation. Fragility functions linking pulse intensity measures to engineering demand parameters will be developed.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, open-source methodology within OpenSRA for quantifying pulse-induced damage risk from frequent, smaller earthquakes. It will deliver fragility curves and practical guidelines for integrating this hazard into urban seismic risk assessments, moving beyond a sole focus on rare, large events. The results will inform potential updates to code provisions for stiff structures in moderate seismicity regions.
proposals.novelty: While velocity pulses are studied for large earthquakes and tall, flexible buildings, their targeted impact on common stiff structures from frequent, smaller events is a novel risk paradigm. The integration of effective stress site response analysis for cohesive soils with structural sliding surface models within a performance-based framework specifically for this hazard scenario is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Performance-Based Assessment of Differential Settlement for Structures on Sloping, Liquefiable Ground
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment frameworks for liquefaction, such as those implemented in OpenSRA, often assume level-ground conditions. This simplification is inadequate for widespread real-world scenarios, including building sites on natural slopes, terraces, or sites with significant subsurface spatial variability. Ignoring ground inclination and soil layer heterogeneity can lead to inaccurate predictions of post-liquefaction differential settlement and tilt, which are critical for assessing damage to infrastructure and buildings.
proposals.hypothesis: The severity and pattern of post-earthquake differential settlement for structures on liquefiable soil are governed by the coupled effects of ground slope, spatial variability of soil layers, and the evolving state of soil resistance during seismic shaking, which can be captured through effective stress analysis within a performance-based framework.
proposals.method: The research will extend Bray's prior effective stress analysis and performance-based methods. We will develop a modular workflow within the OpenSRA software architecture to handle 2D/3D subsurface models with sloping layers and spatially variable soil properties. The core analysis will employ nonlinear effective stress finite difference or finite element models (e.g., FLAC or OpenSees with PM4Sand/PM4Silt constitutive models) to simulate coupled seismic response, pore pressure generation, and post-liquefaction deformation. A sliding-block-inspired analysis will be adapted to assess downslope movement potential, and Monte Carlo simulations will quantify the probabilistic distribution of differential settlement given input variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source methodology for quantifying differential settlement risk on non-level sites, directly enhancing the fidelity of tools like OpenSRA. It will deliver new fragility models linking ground motion intensity to differential settlement severity, providing engineers with critical data for performance-based design and retrofit decisions in complex topographic and geologic settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating a performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) framework, which typically uses 1D column analysis, with 2D/3D effective stress modeling of sloping, heterogeneous ground specifically for building settlement assessment, moving beyond the level-ground and sliding-only failure modes prevalent in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Shallow Foundation Uplift and Gap Formation in Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of foundations on liquefiable soils, as implemented in tools like OpenSRA, typically assumes full contact at the soil-footing interface. This assumption is violated during strong shaking, where foundation uplift and gap formation can occur, significantly altering dynamic soil-structure interaction and post-liquefaction settlement. Ignoring this mechanism may lead to non-conservative estimates of foundation rotation and permanent displacement, compromising the resilience of critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the potential for gap formation and uplift at the soil-footing interface during seismic shaking and liquefaction will lead to more accurate predictions of foundation performance, particularly in terms of permanent tilt and settlement, compared to models enforcing full contact.
proposals.method: The research will extend the effective stress-based numerical modeling framework used in Bray's prior work on liquefaction ejecta. We will implement a zero-tension, frictional contact interface algorithm within a finite difference or finite element code (e.g., FLAC or OpenSees). The constitutive model for the underlying soil will be a validated, pressure-dependent multi-yield surface plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand). The analysis will simulate a suite of recorded ground motions, systematically varying factors such as footing bearing pressure, initial static shear stress, and liquefaction susceptibility. Performance will be quantified using engineering demand parameters like permanent settlement and rotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated modeling strategy for a critical but often neglected failure mode for shallow foundations in liquefiable ground. It will deliver practical guidance on when interface separation must be considered in performance-based engineering assessments, directly enhancing the physical realism of tools like OpenSRA for slope and infrastructure systems.
proposals.novelty: While soil-structure interaction with uplift has been studied for non-liquefied soils, its systematic integration into performance-based assessment frameworks for liquefying soils, where loss of bearing capacity and large deformations interact with gap dynamics, represents a significant advance beyond the state-of-practice.

proposals.title: Validation of Performance-Based Settlement Models for Buildings on Liquefiable Ground Using Centrifuge and Case History Data
proposals.motivation: Performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) for buildings on liquefiable soils relies heavily on analytical models to predict permanent settlement, a key damage state. However, the predictive trends of these models, often derived from simplified effective stress analyses or sliding-block concepts, lack rigorous validation against high-quality physical data. This gap undermines confidence in risk assessments for critical infrastructure, as evidenced by recent frameworks like OpenSRA which require well-calibrated fragility functions.
proposals.hypothesis: The settlement trends predicted by state-of-practice effective stress analysis and sliding-block models show systematic biases when compared to high-quality centrifuge test data and well-documented field case histories, particularly for medium to high severity shaking.
proposals.method: The research will first compile a curated database of building settlement from (1) centrifuge tests (e.g., from the NEES/UCSD and CU Boulder databases) and (2) well-instrumented case histories (e.g., from the 2010-2011 Canterbury sequence). This data will be compared against predictions from two established analytical frameworks: a coupled effective stress analysis (using a constitutive model like PM4Sand) implemented in OpenSees or FLAC, and a performance-based sliding displacement method. Key parameters (e.g., factor of safety against liquefaction, shaking intensity, building contact pressure) will be used to stratify the comparison and quantify model bias and uncertainty via statistical regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available benchmark dataset and quantitative bias factors for settlement models, directly improving the calibration of fragility functions used in tools like OpenSRA. It will provide clear guidance on the range of applicability and limitations of current analytical methods for performance-based assessment of liquefaction-induced building settlement.
proposals.novelty: While individual models and datasets exist, a systematic, paired validation focusing on the *trends* across a wide parameter space—linking advanced centrifuge data with field observations—is lacking. This approach moves beyond single-case calibration to test model generality, a critical step for reliable probabilistic risk assessment.

proposals.title: A Discrete Element Method Framework for Particle-Scale Simulation of Soil-Structure Interaction During Seismic Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of infrastructure on liquefiable ground, as in the PI's OpenSRA work, relies heavily on continuum constitutive models. These models often fail to capture the complex particle-scale rearrangements and force chain dynamics that govern the initiation and severity of liquefaction-induced deformations, such as building settlement and tilt. A fundamental, particle-scale understanding is needed to improve the predictive accuracy of effective stress analyses for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The discrete element method (DEM), calibrated with element test data, can explicitly simulate the transition from a solid-like to a fluid-like state in saturated granular soils beneath a structure, revealing how microscopic particle interactions and force chain evolution control macroscopic sliding and settlement performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D DEM model using the open-source LIGGGHTS or Yade-DEM framework, incorporating a simplified hydrodynamic drag model (e.g., a Stokes-type law) to approximate pore fluid effects during undrained cyclic loading. The model will be calibrated against cyclic direct simple shear and triaxial test data on Ottawa sand to match macroscopic stress-strain and pore pressure response. A rigid foundation element will be explicitly modeled to study soil-structure interaction, with simulations run on high-performance computing clusters to achieve representative element volumes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, particle-scale explanation for key phenomena in liquefaction-induced soil-structure interaction, such as the development of non-uniform deformations and ratcheting settlements. The insights will inform the development of more physically based macroscopic constitutive models for use in continuum effective stress analysis tools, ultimately improving the reliability of performance-based seismic assessment.
proposals.novelty: While DEM is established in granular physics and powder mechanics, its rigorous application to model the full cyclic stress path and soil-structure interaction problem of seismic liquefaction under a building foundation, with calibration to standard geotechnical laboratory tests, represents a significant and non-trivial advancement beyond typical continuum modeling approaches.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Fragility Curves for Building Settlement on Liquefiable Ground Using Effective Stress Seismic Response Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) frameworks often treat liquefaction-induced building settlement as a deterministic consequence of a binary liquefaction trigger. This fails to capture the continuous, severity-dependent nature of settlement, a key damage state for building functionality and safety. A probabilistic demand model linking ground motion intensity to settlement severity is needed to integrate this critical failure mode into seismic risk assessments for infrastructure, as highlighted by recent tools like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: Fragility curves for building settlement on liquefiable soil can be robustly developed using a fully-coupled effective stress analysis within a probabilistic seismic demand model (PSDM) framework, and the resulting curves will show a strong dependence on both the seismic intensity and the pre-earthquake soil state (e.g., relative density, initial static shear stress).
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage computational framework. First, a suite of nonlinear, fully-coupled effective stress dynamic analyses (using a constitutive model like PM4Sand) will be performed for a range of representative soil profiles and building foundation types, subjected to a scaled set of ground motion records. Second, the results will be used to construct probabilistic seismic demand models (PSDMs) via cloud analysis or incremental dynamic analysis (IDA), relating intensity measures (e.g., Sa(T1), PGA) to engineering demand parameters (peak excess pore pressure ratio and permanent settlement). Finally, fragility curves will be derived by applying a lognormal distribution to the probability of exceeding defined settlement thresholds (e.g., 5, 15, 30 cm), conditioned on the intensity measure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a directly applicable fragility framework for quantifying the risk of building settlement due to liquefaction, moving beyond trigger-based assessments. It will provide a critical component for integrating liquefaction consequences into performance-based seismic assessment of buildings and distributed infrastructure systems, enhancing tools like OpenSRA. The study will also elucidate the sensitivity of settlement fragility to key soil state parameters.
proposals.novelty: While fragility curves exist for liquefaction triggering, this proposal uniquely focuses on developing fragility for the consequential engineering demand parameter—building settlement—by leveraging advanced effective stress analysis within a formal PSDM framework, explicitly linking seismic intensity to continuous settlement severity.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Validation of Grain-Scale Mechanisms in Soil-Structure Interaction During Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Advanced numerical models, such as Discrete Element Method (DEM) coupled with pore-fluid solvers, have identified critical grain-scale mechanisms (e.g., force chain evolution, localized dilation) governing soil-structure interaction during seismic liquefaction. However, the validation of these micro-mechanical insights at the macro-scale in geotechnical engineering remains a significant gap, limiting confidence in performance-based assessments of critical infrastructure like slopes and pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: The grain-scale mechanisms (force chain buckling, localized void redistribution) predicted by high-fidelity numerical simulations are the primary drivers of the macro-scale seismic performance (displacement, pore pressure response) observed in physical models of soil-structure systems.
proposals.method: A centrifuge testing program will be designed to physically model soil-structure systems (e.g., a shallow foundation on saturated sand) under seismic excitation. The program will utilize advanced in-flight instrumentation, including tactile pressure sensors and high-resolution Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV), to capture grain-scale stress fields and deformation patterns. The physical model geometry, soil state (relative density, initial stress), and input motion will be explicitly designed to match the boundary conditions of prior 3D coupled DEM-CFD simulations conducted by the PI, enabling direct, point-by-point validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, grain-scale validation dataset for advanced constitutive and numerical models used in liquefaction analysis. It will directly inform and improve the performance-based seismic assessment frameworks, like OpenSRA, by grounding their mechanistic models in physically observed phenomena, leading to more reliable risk assessments for infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted, hypothesis-driven centrifuge experiment designed explicitly to validate specific micro-mechanisms from numerical models, moving beyond traditional macro-response calibration to a fundamental verification of simulated grain-scale physics.

proposals.title: A Portfolio-Based Framework for Probabilistic Liquefaction-Induced Settlement Risk Assessment of Urban Building Blocks
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) for liquefaction primarily focuses on single-site or single-structure analysis. For urban planners, utility operators, and portfolio managers, this scale is insufficient for making cost-effective mitigation and retrofit decisions across entire neighborhoods or infrastructure networks. A scalable, computationally efficient method is needed to aggregate building-specific settlement risk, accounting for spatial variability in soil properties and ground motion, to inform community resilience strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatially-correlated post-liquefaction volumetric and shear-induced settlements for a portfolio of buildings can be efficiently modeled within a probabilistic framework, and the aggregate risk for the portfolio is not a simple sum of individual building risks due to shared geotechnical and seismic hazards.
proposals.method: The method extends the OpenSRA platform's effective stress analysis capabilities. We will implement a modular workflow where: 1) A geostatistical model (e.g., Gaussian Random Field with exponential covariance) generates spatially correlated CPT-based liquefaction triggering and settlement estimates across the urban block. 2) Building footprints are overlaid, and structure-specific settlements are calculated using pre-2026 simplified mechanics-based models (e.g., Zhang et al., 2002) or empirical SPT/CPT-based procedures, incorporating building load effects. 3) A Monte Carlo simulation, integrating ground motion variability via Conditional Spectrum analysis, propagates uncertainties to produce probabilistic settlement hazard curves for each building and loss-exceedance curves for the portfolio.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, scalable tool for portfolio-level settlement risk assessment directly tied to geospatial liquefaction analysis. It will deliver a practical methodology for ranking risk within a building inventory and identifying critical clusters, moving beyond single-asset assessment. The results will directly inform regional seismic risk models and pre-event mitigation planning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of spatial random field modeling of liquefaction severity with portfolio-scale performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE), creating a bridge between detailed geotechnical analysis and urban-scale risk management that was largely unexplored in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Performance-Based Optimization of Ground Improvement for Liquefaction-Induced Settlement Using Effective Stress Analysis and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced ground settlement poses a significant risk to infrastructure during earthquakes. Current ground improvement design often relies on simplified, prescriptive methods that may not efficiently target the most critical settlement mechanisms, leading to either costly over-design or insufficient performance. A performance-based, optimization-driven framework is needed to design cost-effective, resilient ground improvement schemes for critical assets like natural gas pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating advanced effective stress analysis (e.g., PM4Sand, OpenSees) with gradient-free optimization algorithms, ground improvement schemes can be systematically designed to minimize post-liquefaction settlement by targeting the specific soil layers and mechanisms (e.g., volumetric reconsolidation, shear-induced deformation) that dominate the system's performance.
proposals.method: The research will develop a computational framework linking OpenSRA's performance assessment modules with a design optimization loop. A high-fidelity, 2D effective stress model of a liquefiable soil profile will be constructed. Ground improvement parameters (e.g., column spacing, depth, and stiffness) will be design variables. A multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) will minimize total settlement and improvement cost, subject to performance constraints. The framework will use Python for orchestration, with OpenSees for nonlinear dynamic analysis and scikit-learn for surrogate modeling to accelerate the optimization process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source computational tool for performance-based geotechnical design, moving beyond current factor-of-safety approaches. It will provide a systematic methodology to identify the most efficient ground improvement configuration for a given seismic hazard and soil profile, directly linking improvement design to quantified performance metrics like settlement.
proposals.novelty: While effective stress analysis is used for assessment, its direct integration with multi-objective optimization to *design* ground improvement is a significant advance. The novelty lies in framing ground improvement design as an inverse problem, where the model identifies the critical failure mechanisms and automatically tailors the solution.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Seismic Slope Stability Analysis Incorporating Spatially Variable and Layered Soil Properties
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessments of slopes, including those in tools like OpenSRA, often rely on simplified homogeneous or equivalent soil profiles. This neglects the inherent spatial variability of soil properties (e.g., relative density, fines content, permeability) and layering, which critically control localized liquefaction triggering, pore pressure redistribution, and the development of non-uniform deformation and sliding mechanisms. Ignoring this heterogeneity can lead to inaccurate predictions of displacement severity and failure mode, undermining risk assessments for infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling spatially variable and layered soil properties within a fully-coupled effective stress framework will significantly alter the predicted deformation mechanism, localization of shear strains, and overall performance (displacement) of slopes under seismic loading compared to homogeneous assumptions.
proposals.method: The research will extend Bray's existing effective stress analysis workflows by implementing a stochastic field model within OpenSRA or a similar finite difference/element platform (e.g., FLAC, OpenSees). Key soil parameters (e.g., (N1)60cs, permeability) will be modeled as correlated random fields using the Karhunen-Loève expansion or sequential Gaussian simulation, constrained by site investigation data. Layered stratigraphy will be explicitly defined. A suite of nonlinear dynamic analyses will be performed on slopes with both heterogeneous and homogenized properties, using validated constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, PM4Silt) to compare triggering patterns, deformation mechanisms, and sliding block-derived displacements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, geotechnically defensible methodology for incorporating soil heterogeneity into performance-based seismic slope assessment. It will deliver practical guidance on when spatial variability is most consequential for risk and will advance the state of practice beyond simplified sliding block models by linking heterogeneous property fields to complex failure mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While random field methods exist in static geotechnical reliability, their integration into fully-coupled, dynamic effective stress analysis for performance-based seismic slope assessment represents a significant advancement, moving beyond deterministic or homogeneous liquefaction evaluations toward a more realistic representation of in-situ conditions.

proposals.title: A Multi-Phase u-p-U Formulation for Seismic Slope Performance: Integrating Air Phase Dynamics in Liquefaction-Induced Landslide Simulation
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of slopes, particularly for liquefiable soils, often relies on effective stress analysis treating the soil as a saturated two-phase (soil-water) medium. This simplification neglects the role of the air phase, which can significantly influence pore pressure generation, dissipation, and the resulting shear strength and deformation mechanisms during strong shaking. A more physically accurate representation is needed to reduce uncertainties in predicting the severity of flow failures and sediment ejecta, which are critical for infrastructure risk assessment tools like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the soil as a three-phase mixture (soil grains, pore water, and pore air) using a coupled u-p-U formulation will yield significantly different predictions of seismic slope displacement and post-shaking stability compared to conventional saturated two-phase or single-phase analyses, particularly for partially saturated or loosely deposited soils where air entrapment occurs.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing OpenSRA framework by implementing a three-phase dynamic finite element solver based on the u-p-U formulation, where u is solid displacement, p is pore water pressure, and U is pore air pressure. The constitutive model for the solid skeleton will incorporate a state-dependent, critical-state compatible plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand or SANISAND) modified to account for the influence of degree of saturation on dilatancy and liquefaction resistance. The coupled hydro-mechanical equations will be solved using a staggered implicit time-integration scheme. Validation will be performed against centrifuge test data for partially saturated slopes and documented case histories of seismic landslides.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source numerical capability for performance-based assessment of slopes that captures the full spectrum of soil saturation states. It will deliver new insights into how air phase compressibility modulates liquefaction triggering and post-liquefaction shear deformation, directly improving the physical basis for seismic hazard and risk evaluations of natural gas infrastructure and other assets on sloping ground.
proposals.novelty: While three-phase formulations exist in geomechanics literature, their integration into a performance-based seismic assessment framework for slope systems, and specifically within an open-source platform like OpenSRA for practical risk analysis, represents a significant advancement beyond the state-of-practice saturated or single-phase representations.

proposals.title: A Parametric Framework for Quantifying Strain-Softening Effects on Seismically-Induced Landslide Runout Using Effective Stress Analysis
proposals.motivation: Post-liquefaction and seismic landslide runout remains a major source of infrastructure damage and hazard, but predictive models often rely on simplified soil strength parameters. The transition from peak to residual strength—strain-softening—is a critical yet poorly quantified control on ultimate mobility. A systematic, parametric understanding of this softening behavior is needed to improve performance-based assessments of slope systems, particularly for critical infrastructure like natural gas networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The runout distance and dynamics of seismically triggered landslides are dominantly controlled by the rate and magnitude of post-peak strength loss (strain-softening), which can be characterized by a small set of normalized constitutive parameters governing the effective stress path.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage numerical modeling framework. First, we will use OpenSRA-compatible effective stress site response analysis (e.g., PM4Sand) to simulate seismic triggering and initial softening at multiple representative slope configurations. Second, we will implement the resulting spatially varied strength states as initial conditions in a Material Point Method (MPM) code to simulate the large-deformation runout phase. A parametric study will systematically vary key softening parameters (e.g., brittleness index, dilation angle, critical state friction angle) within ranges established from pre-2026 laboratory data to generate a database of runout outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, physics-based correlation between fundamental soil constitutive parameters and landslide mobility metrics (runout length, velocity, deposit geometry). It will directly enhance the performance-based seismic assessment of slopes by providing a more rigorous link between site-specific soil state and hazard severity. The resulting parametric relationships will be formatted for potential integration into probabilistic risk platforms like OpenSRA.
proposals.novelty: While strain-softening is known to be important, this study uniquely integrates state-of-the-art effective stress triggering analysis with advanced runout simulation to *quantitatively* isolate and rank the influence of specific softening parameters on dynamic mobility, moving beyond empirical or rigid-block models.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Seismic Liquefaction Potential of Gravelly Engineered Fills: A Performance-Based Effective Stress Framework
proposals.motivation: Current seismic risk assessment practice, including codes and tools like OpenSRA, often categorically assumes gravelly fills are non-liquefiable. This assumption underpins the safety evaluation of critical infrastructure like dams, embankments, and port facilities. However, field case histories and limited laboratory data suggest that loose, saturated, gravelly soils with sufficient fines content may be susceptible to cyclic softening and flow deformation under strong shaking, posing a potentially unquantified risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Gravelly engineered fills, particularly those with significant fines content (e.g., >15%) and in a loose, saturated state, can experience seismically-induced excess pore pressure generation leading to strength loss and large deformation, challenging the blanket 'non-liquefiable' classification.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled experimental and numerical performance-based framework. First, a database of historical cases and published cyclic simple shear/triaxial tests on gravelly soils will be compiled. New laboratory testing on reconstituted specimens will target state-dependent cyclic resistance. These data will inform the calibration of a critical state-based, bounding surface plasticity constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand/PM4Silt framework extended for gravelly soils) within the OpenSees platform. The model will be used in 2D/3D effective stress site-response and sliding block analyses of representative slope and fill systems to quantify deformation hazards.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, state-dependent framework for assessing gravelly fill performance, moving beyond binary liquefaction triggers. It will deliver calibrated constitutive model parameters and practical guidelines for integrating gravelly soil susceptibility into performance-based seismic assessment tools like OpenSRA, leading to more reliable risk estimates for infrastructure founded on or within such fills.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically challenging a deeply entrenched simplifying assumption in practice by applying state-of-the-art critical state soil mechanics and effective stress analysis, typically reserved for sands and silts, to the gravelly fill domain. It directly links material state to system-level performance for these soils.

proposals.title: Causal Attribution of Seismic Damage in Reclaimed Lands: Isolating the Mechanistic Role of Fill Placement Fabric through Integrated Experimentation and Numerical Prototyping
proposals.motivation: Modern infrastructure is increasingly built on reclaimed land, yet post-earthquake damage assessments struggle to disentangle the causal influence of fill placement method from confounding factors like soil composition and ground motion characteristics. This ambiguity hinders the development of performance-based seismic design and retrofit guidelines for these critical zones. A causal, mechanics-based understanding is needed to move beyond empirical correlations and enable predictive assessment of liquefaction and deformation.
proposals.hypothesis: The method of fill placement (e.g., hydraulic, end-dumping, compaction) causally determines the soil fabric (density, stratification, anisotropy), which in turn directly governs the severity and pattern of liquefaction-induced damage under seismic loading, independent of other material properties.
proposals.method: The research employs a three-pillar causal inference framework. First, intrinsic properties of a uniform fill material are characterized via advanced laboratory tests (e.g., cyclic simple shear, bender elements). Second, high-fidelity, 3D effective stress finite element models (using platforms like OpenSees or PLAXIS) are used to create synthetic 'virtual prototypes' where identical soil properties are assigned, but the initial fabric state is varied systematically to mimic placement methods. Third, these fabric states are physically replicated in a geotechnical centrifuge for validation, using precise pluviation and compaction techniques to create instrumented model fills subjected to controlled seismic motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, predictive framework that quantifies the causal link between construction practice and seismic performance. It will deliver mechanics-based fragility relationships for different reclamation methods, directly informing performance-based assessment tools like OpenSRA. The results will provide a scientific basis for specifying construction methods to mitigate liquefaction risk.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond statistical association by employing a virtual prototyping and controlled physical modeling strategy to isolate fabric as a causal variable, a significant advance over traditional forensic analyses of case histories where variables are conflated. It formally integrates causal reasoning from data science into geotechnical earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: Linking Reclamation Construction Methods to Seismic Performance: A Quantitative Framework for Predicting Liquefaction-Induced Damage Patterns
proposals.motivation: Engineered fill sites, such as reclaimed land for ports and infrastructure, are highly vulnerable to liquefaction during earthquakes, as demonstrated in events like the 2010-2011 Canterbury sequence. Pre-2026 practice often treats these fills as homogeneous, yet their construction method (e.g., end-dumping, hydraulic placement) fundamentally controls soil fabric, density, and layering, which dictate seismic performance. A quantitative link between construction technique and expected damage is missing, hindering risk-informed design and retrofit prioritization.
proposals.hypothesis: The specific construction method used for land reclamation imposes a distinct, quantifiable soil fabric and stratigraphy that governs the onset, severity, and spatial pattern of liquefaction and its associated ground failure (e.g., lateral spreading, settlement, ejecta).
proposals.method: The research will integrate case history analysis with advanced numerical modeling. First, a database of well-documented fill sites will be compiled, categorizing construction method, inferred soil state parameters, and observed seismic damage. Second, site-specific, 2D/3D effective stress finite element models (using OpenSees or PLAXIS with PM4Sand/PM4Silt constitutive models) will be developed for representative sites. The models will explicitly simulate the depositional layering and fabric effects associated with each construction method. The framework will be implemented within a performance-based assessment workflow, extending tools like OpenSRA to incorporate construction-method-specific fragility relationships for sliding displacement and ejecta severity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative framework for predicting liquefaction damage patterns based on reclamation construction method. It will deliver new, method-specific fragility functions for lateral spreading and ejecta, directly usable in performance-based engineering and risk assessment platforms. The results will provide actionable guidance for the seismic evaluation of existing reclaimed land and for selecting construction methods for new reclamation projects.
proposals.novelty: While soil state is known to influence liquefaction, this proposal uniquely ties the causative construction process directly to spatial damage patterns through advanced constitutive modeling of fabric and stratigraphy, moving beyond simplified, homogenized characterizations of fill. It integrates geotechnical construction practice with performance-based earthquake engineering in a novel, mechanistic way.

proposals.title: Performance-Based Assessment of Building Resilience to Differential Settlement in Laterally Extending versus Uniformly Settling Ground
proposals.motivation: Post-liquefaction ground deformation poses a severe threat to building foundations, but current performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) frameworks often treat settlement as a uniform vertical displacement. In reality, lateral spreading and ground oscillation can induce significant differential settlement and lateral extension, imposing complex kinematic loads on foundations. A direct comparison of building performance under these distinct deformation modes is needed to improve risk assessment for structures in liquefiable zones.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given magnitude of vertical settlement, building damage states and repair costs will be significantly more severe under the differential settlement and lateral extension characteristic of lateral spreading compared to uniform settlement, due to the combined effects of tilt, distortion, and induced structural moments.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled effective stress site response and structural analysis framework, building on the PI's prior work in OpenSRA. We will use FLAC or OpenSees to simulate soil-structure interaction for prototype buildings on shallow foundations, subjecting them to suites of ground motions that trigger liquefaction. The analysis will explicitly model two post-shaking ground deformation scenarios: 1) uniform consolidation settlement, and 2) lateral extension with differential settlement. Building performance metrics (interstory drift, foundation rotation, and strain-based damage indices) will be computed using performance-based assessment methodologies and compared statistically.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative fragility functions that distinguish between damage due to differential and uniform settlement, directly informing next-generation seismic risk assessment platforms like OpenSRA. It will deliver practical guidance for prioritizing retrofit strategies in zones susceptible to lateral spreading versus deeper, uniform settlement.
proposals.novelty: While differential settlement is known to be damaging, this study provides a controlled, performance-based quantification of its added severity relative to uniform settlement for building superstructures, explicitly linking specific post-liquefaction ground deformation mechanisms to structural engineering damage states.

proposals.title: Integrating InSAR-Derived Deformation with Performance-Based Seismic Assessment for Port Slopes and Reclaimed Land
proposals.motivation: Ports are critical, high-value infrastructure hubs often built on liquefaction-prone, reclaimed soils. Pre-2026, post-earthquake damage assessments at ports are typically ground-based, localized, and slow, hindering rapid response and system-wide resilience planning. A method to rapidly quantify spatially continuous, pre- and post-event ground deformation patterns across entire port facilities is needed to calibrate and validate performance-based seismic models for these complex soil-structure systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That differential ground deformation patterns at port facilities, mapped via multi-temporal InSAR, correlate systematically with pre-event geotechnical state parameters (e.g., CPT-based liquefaction resistance) and can be used to calibrate the sliding displacement models in performance-based seismic assessment frameworks like OpenSRA.
proposals.method: We will process Sentinel-1 and ALOS-2 InSAR data using the Stanford Method for Persistent Scatterers (StaMPS) to generate high-resolution time series of pre-event subsidence and co-seismic/post-seismic deformation for a case-study port. These deformation maps will be spatially registered with pre-event geotechnical data (CPT soundings, boreholes). We will then use the OpenSRA framework to conduct effective stress-based, performance-based assessments of port slopes and quay walls, using the InSAR-measured deformations as calibration targets for key model parameters controlling lateral spreading and settlement severity.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, data-driven protocol for validating and calibrating seismic performance models for port infrastructure using remote sensing. It will directly enhance the reliability of tools like OpenSRA for port risk assessment. The resulting methodology will provide a template for rapid, post-earthquake forensic analysis and pre-event resilience benchmarking of distributed infrastructure systems.
proposals.novelty: While InSAR has been used for deformation monitoring, its integration as a quantitative calibration dataset for performance-based seismic assessment of port-scale geotechnical systems, specifically linking sliding displacement to pre-event soil state, is a novel advancement beyond current practice.

proposals.title: Regional Seismic Fragility Models for Port Infrastructure Integrating Site-Specific Liquefaction and Sliding Performance
proposals.motivation: Ports are critical, complex nodes in global supply chains, yet their seismic vulnerability is poorly quantified at a regional scale. Pre-2026 risk assessments for ports often rely on generic fragility functions that inadequately capture the coupled performance of wharves, cranes, and storage yards under liquefaction-induced ground failure. A scalable, performance-based framework is urgently needed to inform resilience planning for maritime transportation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Regional-scale seismic fragility models for port infrastructure can be robustly developed by integrating site-specific, effective-stress-based liquefaction severity estimates with sliding displacement analyses for wharf and crane systems, thereby capturing the dominant failure modes observed in past earthquakes.
proposals.method: The method extends the OpenSRA platform's modular architecture. First, we will implement a regional-scale probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) driver. For each site, the 'Effective Stress Analysis of Liquefiable Sites' module will estimate ejecta severity and pore pressure rise. These outputs will feed into a new 'Sliding Displacement' module, using Bray & Travasarou-type rigid-block analyses calibrated for wharf decks and crane rails. The final 'Fragility Integrator' will combine component performances via logic trees, generating system-level fragility curves (probability of exceeding damage states vs. intensity measure) for archetypical port configurations across the study region.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source, regionally applicable fragility library for port infrastructure that explicitly links liquefaction severity to component and system performance. It will provide a critical tool for portfolio risk assessment (e.g., for the U.S. Department of Transportation) and establish a transparent methodology that can be updated with new case histories or component models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, large-scale integration of Bray's recent work on ejecta severity and sliding performance into a unified, automated regional assessment framework, moving beyond single-site analysis to generate spatially distributed fragility surfaces essential for network resilience analysis.

proposals.title: Inversion of Co-seismic Surface Rupture Patterns for Subsurface Soil Density Profiles: A Performance-Based Geotechnical Framework
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake site investigations for liquefaction and ground failure are costly, invasive, and spatially limited.
