A method to estimate the in-situ soil density profile—a critical state parameter controlling liquefaction resistance and shear strength—from readily observable surface deformation would revolutionize rapid seismic performance assessment and infrastructure risk evaluation, particularly for distributed systems like natural gas networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial pattern and magnitude of permanent ground deformation (e.g., lateral spreading, settlement, fissuring) following an earthquake contain sufficient information to inversely constrain the pre-event soil density profile, as density is a primary control on the dynamic stress-strain response and post-liquefaction strength of soil layers.
proposals.method: The approach will couple a forward model with an inverse optimization framework. The forward model will use effective stress, nonlinear finite element analysis (e.g., OpenSees with PM4Sand/PM4Silt constitutive models) to simulate seismic response and permanent deformation for hypothesized soil columns. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian updating framework (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) or an ensemble smoother, where observed surface rupture metrics (from lidar or satellite InSAR) are the data, and the uncertain parameters are layer-by-layer relative density. The OpenSRA platform will be adapted to host this inversion workflow.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, non-invasive methodology for estimating in-situ density, a key geotechnical parameter. It will advance performance-based seismic assessment by providing a data-assimilation tool to calibrate subsurface models from surface observations, directly improving the accuracy of regional liquefaction hazard maps and infrastructure risk analyses like those in OpenSRA.
proposals.novelty: While inverse problems exist in geophysics (e.g., for shear wave velocity), directly targeting soil density—a more fundamental engineering property—from surface rupture patterns is unexplored. The novelty lies in integrating high-fidelity effective stress constitutive models within a Bayesian inversion scheme specifically for this purpose.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Distributed Ground Deformation versus Discrete Rupture in Seismic Hazard for Infrastructure on Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment for infrastructure on liquefiable soils often focuses on discrete ground rupture or localized liquefaction as primary hazards. However, field observations from events like the 2011 Christchurch earthquake suggest that distributed, non-uniform ground deformation over a broad zone, driven by soil softening and lateral spreading, can cause severe damage even without a rupture plane directly beneath an asset. This mechanism is not well quantified in existing risk frameworks like OpenSRA, potentially leading to an underestimation of hazard for distributed infrastructure systems.
proposals.hypothesis: For infrastructure on level or gently sloping liquefiable ground, the seismic hazard and resulting damage are dominated by distributed, inelastic shear deformation and differential settlement across a broad zone, rather than by the presence of a distinct tectonic surface rupture directly beneath the structure.
proposals.method: The research will employ 2D and 3D effective stress finite element analysis (using platforms like OpenSees or FLAC) to model soil-structure systems subjected to seismic shaking. A key component is the implementation and calibration of advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) capable of capturing post-liquefaction large deformation and cyclic softening. We will systematically compare scenarios with and without a modeled discrete fault rupture, instead applying a kinematic boundary condition representing distributed subsurface shear strain. The analysis will use performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) methodology, computing engineering demand parameters (EDPs) like lateral displacement and differential settlement for generic pipeline and shallow foundation systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework for incorporating distributed ground deformation hazards into performance-based assessments, directly informing tools like OpenSRA. It will yield practical guidelines for when discrete rupture analysis is sufficient versus when a distributed deformation analysis is required, improving the accuracy of risk estimates for gas pipelines, transportation networks, and other linear infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction-induced lateral spreading is recognized, this study explicitly decouples and quantifies the hazard from broad zone 'twisting' versus discrete rupture, challenging the default assumption that the most severe hazard requires a rupture directly beneath an asset. It integrates state-of-the-art constitutive modeling with PBEE specifically to address this conceptual gap.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Scaling of Tabular Rupture Zone Width with Earthquake Magnitude and Its Implications for Site-Specific Liquefaction Hazard
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of infrastructure, such as natural gas pipelines or slope systems, often relies on simplified models of fault rupture propagation. The width of the tabular rupture zone is a critical, yet poorly constrained, parameter for predicting permanent ground deformation and the severity of sediment ejecta at liquefiable sites. Understanding its scaling with earthquake magnitude is essential for improving the geotechnical inputs to risk assessment platforms like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effective width of the tabular rupture zone does not scale linearly with seismic moment but is instead modulated by near-surface material state and stress conditions, leading to greater variability and potential for wider zones in soft, saturated soils prone to liquefaction.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of well-documented surface ruptures from earthquakes (M5.0-8.0), extracting rupture zone width from field studies and high-resolution geodetic data (InSAR, lidar). Using a Bayesian statistical framework, we will model width as a function of magnitude, depth, and near-surface shear-wave velocity (Vs30). The analysis will be integrated with effective stress-based site response simulations to assess how predicted rupture widths influence the computed severity of cyclic softening and post-liquefaction sliding deformations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically grounded scaling relationship for rupture zone width that incorporates site-specific geotechnical conditions. It will directly enhance the physical realism of fault displacement inputs for performance-based assessments in OpenSRA and improve the estimation of off-fault deformation hazards for critical infrastructure on liquefiable ground.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for rupture length and slip, the systematic quantification of rupture zone width—particularly its dependence on near-surface material state—remains an open problem. This study uniquely bridges seismological source characterization with geotechnical engineering practice for liquefaction hazard assessment.

proposals.title: A Continuum Damage Mechanics Framework for Simulating Fault Rupture and Its Impact on Near-Field Ground Motions
proposals.motivation: Current physics-based earthquake rupture models often treat faults as discrete frictional surfaces, which can oversimplify the complex, damaged shear zone architecture observed in field studies. This simplification limits the ability to model key near-field phenomena like high-frequency radiation and permanent ground deformation, which are critical for performance-based seismic assessment of infrastructure. Integrating a material science description of the fault core as a damaged continuum can bridge this gap, offering a more physically realistic basis for ground motion prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the tabular fault core as a continuum with evolving anisotropic damage, governed by a scalar damage variable and a tensorial damage descriptor, can effectively simulate the transition from distributed inelastic deformation to localized rupture, thereby improving predictions of near-field ground shaking and permanent displacement.
proposals.method: We will implement a coupled finite element framework in OpenSees or a similar platform, extending Bray's prior effective stress and performance-based work. The fault zone will be modeled using a continuum damage mechanics (CDM) constitutive law, where damage evolution is driven by a modified Mohr-Coulomb yield criterion incorporating plastic strain and a damage energy release rate. The numerical approach will involve: 1) defining initial damage anisotropy based on fault zone structure, 2) implementing a rate-dependent damage evolution law to simulate rupture propagation, and 3) coupling this fault zone element with bounding elastic crustal blocks to simulate full wave propagation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source numerical tool for physics-based fault rupture simulation that explicitly accounts for fault zone damage. It will provide new insights into the generation of near-field seismic demands, directly benefiting performance-based assessment of critical infrastructure like slopes and pipelines, as addressed in OpenSRA. The framework will also offer a quantitative link between geologic fault zone properties and engineering ground motion parameters.
proposals.novelty: While CDM is established in composite materials and concrete research, its application to model the entire earthquake rupture process within a fault core as a damaging continuum, rather than as a discrete interface, is a significant departure from mainstream seismic source mechanics. This approach uniquely integrates geologic structure, inelastic deformation, and dynamic rupture into a single, performance-oriented computational framework.

proposals.title: Real-Time Performance Assessment of Shallow Foundations on Liquefiable Ground Using Embedded Structural Health Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of foundations, especially on liquefiable soils, relies heavily on post-event observations and simplified analytical models. These methods lack validation from direct, high-fidelity measurements of foundation deformation and soil-structure interaction during strong shaking. This gap limits the accuracy of risk assessment tools like OpenSRA for critical infrastructure and hinders the development of resilient design practices.
proposals.hypothesis: That continuous, real-time measurements of foundation deformation and pore pressure via embedded sensor arrays can directly quantify the onset and severity of sliding and settlement mechanisms in liquefiable soils, providing a ground-truth dataset that reveals deficiencies in current effective stress analysis and sliding block models.
proposals.method: We will instrument a series of shallow foundation models in a large geotechnical centrifuge with accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and fiber-optic strain sensors to capture real-time soil-structure interaction during simulated earthquakes. The sensor data will be processed using a Kalman filter algorithm to fuse measurements and estimate foundation displacement vectors. These vectors will be compared against predictions from a parallel numerical model (using OpenSees with PM4Sand constitutive model) and a simplified sliding block analysis to calibrate and validate the numerical frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind, instrumented dataset linking direct foundation response to soil state evolution during liquefaction. It will provide a benchmark for calibrating performance-based assessment tools and improve the physical basis for estimating the severity of ground failure and its impact on infrastructure within risk assessment platforms.
proposals.novelty: While SHM is common for superstructures, its application to directly instrument and analyze the hidden, in-ground response of foundations during liquefaction is novel. This approach moves beyond indirect inference to provide direct empirical validation of coupled effective stress analysis and sliding deformation models.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Performance and Liquefaction Severity of Moderate-Magnitude (M5.5-6.5) Crustal Earthquakes: A High-Resolution Case Study Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 seismic hazard and performance-based assessments are heavily calibrated against large-magnitude (M>6.5) events, where surface rupture and widespread liquefaction are common. However, moderate-magnitude earthquakes (M5.5-6.5) constitute a more frequent hazard to distributed infrastructure like natural gas networks. The detailed performance of soil and slope systems under these smaller, yet damaging, events with localized surface rupture is poorly quantified, creating a gap in risk models for high-probability scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial patterns and severity of ground failure (liquefaction ejecta severity, slope deformation) from moderate-magnitude earthquakes scale non-linearly with magnitude and are strongly controlled by near-surface stratigraphy and pre-event soil state, even when surface rupture length is limited.
proposals.method: We will apply the high-resolution mapping and effective stress analysis methodology from Bray's prior work on liquefaction ejecta to 2-3 well-documented historical M5.5-6.5 crustal earthquakes with surface rupture (e.g., 2014 South Napa, 2011 Prague, OK). For each event, we will integrate pre-2026 tools: 1) using OpenSRA frameworks to define the seismic demand, 2) compiling high-resolution CPT/SPT logs and groundwater data to perform site-specific effective stress analyses, and 3) employing sliding block and performance-based slope assessment methods to back-analyze observed deformations. The core algorithm will be a comparative statistical analysis of predicted vs. observed damage intensity as a function of distance from rupture and geologic unit.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique dataset linking detailed geotechnical characterization to observed ground failure for moderate earthquakes, directly informing performance-based earthquake engineering (PBEE) for more frequent hazard levels. It will enhance the calibration of probabilistic seismic risk assessment platforms like OpenSRA for common, lower-severity but high-probability events, improving infrastructure resilience decisions.
proposals.novelty: While detailed post-event mapping is standard for large earthquakes, its systematic application to quantify the *limits* of liquefaction and slope failure triggering in moderate events is novel. This bridges the gap between large-event case studies and generic ground motion prediction models by providing empirical constraints on the lower-bound magnitude for significant soil failure.

proposals.title: Decoupling Liquefaction Triggering from Surface Manifestation: A Performance-Based Framework for Assessing Subsurface-Confined Liquefaction
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard assessments and models like OpenSRA often assume that soil element liquefaction inevitably leads to observable ground failure or sediment ejecta. This leads to potential over-prediction of damage, particularly for critical, buried infrastructure like natural gas pipelines, where the economic and safety consequences of unnecessary mitigation are significant. A rigorous, mechanics-based framework to identify when liquefaction remains confined is needed for more accurate risk analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Liquefaction triggering at the element level does not necessarily propagate to cause system-level ground failure; the severity of surface manifestation is controlled by the coupled performance of the liquefied layer, the overlying non-liquefiable crust, and the dynamic seismic demand.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's existing effective stress and sliding block frameworks. The approach integrates 1D nonlinear effective stress site response analysis (using a model like PM4Sand) to identify triggering, with a 2D finite difference or discrete element model to simulate post-triggering deformation. A key algorithm will be a coupled stress-redistribution routine that assesses whether the weakened layer can be statically or kinematically confined by the overburden. The analysis will be framed within a performance-based earthquake engineering context, defining limit states for 'confined liquefaction' versus 'manifested failure'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanics-based methodology to distinguish between benign, subsurface-confined liquefaction and hazardous cases leading to ejecta or lateral spreading. It will directly improve the accuracy of seismic risk assessments in tools like OpenSRA by reducing false-positive predictions of ground failure. The framework will offer engineers a rational basis for deciding when mitigation for liquefaction surface effects is truly warranted.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard paradigm by treating surface manifestation as a separable, performance-based outcome rather than a direct consequence of triggering. The novelty lies in explicitly modeling the stress-deformation compatibility between a liquefied layer and its confining crust as a system-level boundary value problem.

proposals.title: A Performance-Based Framework for Quantifying Subsurface Confinement and Drainage as Mitigators of Surface Liquefaction Manifestation
proposals.motivation: Current seismic risk assessments, such as those in OpenSRA, often predict widespread liquefaction surface manifestations (e.g., sand boils, lateral spreading) based on element-level triggering. However, field observations from past earthquakes repeatedly show sites where triggering is predicted but no surface effects occur, leading to potentially costly over-conservatism. This discrepancy undermines confidence in performance-based engineering and complicates risk management for critical infrastructure on liquefiable soils.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the absence of surface manifestation at triggered sites can be systematically explained and quantified by the combined effects of (1) a competent, low-permeability surficial crust that provides confinement and (2) the presence of natural subsurface drainage layers that dissipate excess pore pressures before they reach the surface.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled effective stress analysis framework using OpenSees or a similar platform, building on the PI's prior work. We will develop a parametric 2D finite element model of a soil column with a liquefiable layer, systematically varying the thickness and strength of an overlying non-liquefiable crust and the depth/permeability of embedded drainage layers. The analysis will use a critical-state compatible constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) to simulate cyclic triggering and post-triggering response. The key metric will be the 'Manifestation Potential Index,' calculated as the ratio of upward dissipated energy to the work required to fracture the crust, calibrated against case histories of no-manifestation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, mechanics-based screening tool to identify sites where surface manifestation is unlikely despite element triggering, directly improving the accuracy of tools like OpenSRA. It will advance performance-based assessment by moving beyond binary triggering towards predicting actual ground failure phenotypes, offering a more nuanced risk picture for slope systems and buried infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While the role of a crust is acknowledged, this study uniquely quantifies its interaction with drainage in a performance-based framework, shifting the focus from triggering alone to the integrated system's ability to suppress surface effects—a critical gap between element tests and field performance.

proposals.title: Detection and Performance-Based Assessment of Hidden Liquefaction Using Integrated Geophysical and CPT-Based Effective Stress Analysis
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake observations reveal that liquefaction can occur in subsurface layers without surface manifestations (e.g., sand boils), leading to unaccounted-for ground deformation and damage to buried infrastructure. Current liquefaction hazard assessments, often based on simplified penetration resistance, may miss these 'hidden' layers, resulting in an underestimation of seismic risk for critical systems like natural gas pipelines. This gap is critical for performance-based engineering, which requires accurate characterization of the post-shaking ground state.
proposals.hypothesis: Shear wave velocity (Vs) profiles, particularly when integrated with cone penetration test (CPT) data, can reliably identify soil layers that have undergone liquefaction (i.e., reached a critical state) during shaking but did not manifest ejecta at the surface. The severity of hidden liquefaction can be quantified through effective stress analysis to predict its impact on ground performance.
proposals.method: The research will develop a multi-method field and analytical framework. First, surface-wave geophysical methods (e.g., MASW) will be used at well-documented post-liquefaction sites to derive high-resolution Vs profiles. These Vs profiles will be integrated with pre- and post-event CPTu data to calibrate relationships between Vs, state parameter, and cyclic resistance. The core analysis will employ a modified version of the OpenSRA effective stress analysis framework, implementing a sliding-deformation model for slopes or a coupled soil-structure interaction model for pipelines. The algorithm will use the geophysically-identified 'liquefied state' layers as input to compute permanent ground deformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, field-validated methodology for detecting and accounting for hidden liquefaction in seismic risk assessments. It will advance performance-based practice by linking geophysical indicators of in-situ state to quantitative estimates of ground deformation severity for infrastructure. The outcome will be a practical module or guideline for integration into existing platforms like OpenSRA.
proposals.novelty: While Vs is used for liquefaction triggering evaluation, its targeted application to diagnose the *post-event state* of hidden liquefaction is novel. The integration of surface-wave geophysics as a direct input into a performance-based effective stress analysis framework for ground failure represents a significant advancement beyond current CPT/SPT-centric methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Seismic Demand from Unmanifested Liquefaction Using Effective Stress Analysis and Bayesian Updating
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of liquefiable sites often relies on forward modeling, which can over-predict damage when surface manifestations (e.g., ejecta, lateral spreading) are absent. This discrepancy leads to overly conservative risk estimates for critical infrastructure. An inverse approach is needed to reconcile evidence of triggered liquefaction (from pore pressure sensors or post-event sampling) with the lack of observed damage, providing a more accurate and less biased estimate of the true ground motion demand.
proposals.hypothesis: The true seismic demand at a site where liquefaction was triggered but did not manifest can be accurately estimated by inversely solving a coupled effective stress model, constrained by in-situ post-event soil state data and the observed 'no-failure' performance of the soil or slope system.
proposals.method: The method will leverage the OpenSRA framework and effective stress analysis tools from Bray's prior work. We will develop an inverse solver that couples a constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) with a Bayesian updating algorithm (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo). The forward model simulates liquefaction triggering and post-liquefaction shear deformation. The inverse engine will iteratively adjust the input ground motion time history to match the posterior state (e.g., residual excess pore pressure, shear strain) inferred from field data, while satisfying the performance constraint of no significant sliding or ejecta.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, evidence-based framework for calibrating seismic demand in forensic analysis and reducing epistemic uncertainty in performance-based earthquake engineering. It will yield a practical methodology for validating and refining ground motion predictions at liquefiable sites, directly improving the accuracy of seismic risk assessments for infrastructure like natural gas networks.
proposals.novelty: While forward effective stress analysis is standard, inverting the problem to solve for demand from a 'no manifestation' performance outcome is a significant paradigm shift. The integration of Bayesian inference with high-fidelity effective stress models to constrain seismic demand in this specific context has not been systematically explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Seismic System Response in Stratified Low-Plasticity Silt Deposits: From Pore Pressure Generation to Surface Manifestations
proposals.motivation: Low-plasticity silts are prevalent in many seismically active regions, yet their liquefaction and post-liquefaction behavior remain poorly understood compared to sands. Current simplified procedures, often based on clean sand correlations, may be unconservative or overly conservative for stratified silt profiles, leading to significant uncertainty in performance-based assessments of infrastructure founded on or near such deposits. A system-level experimental investigation is needed to evaluate the coupled effects of pore pressure generation, drainage, and inter-layer interaction on overall ground failure.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic performance of stratified silt deposits is governed not only by the cyclic resistance of individual layers but by the system's ability to dissipate excess pore pressures through drainage boundaries and inter-layer flow, which can either mitigate or exacerbate deformations depending on the stratification sequence.
proposals.method: A series of dynamic centrifuge tests will be conducted on instrumented, stratified models of low-plasticity silt (e.g., Nevada silt) with varying layer thickness, density, and sequencing (e.g., silt layers interbedded with sandy silt or fine sand lenses). Models will be subjected to scaled earthquake motions of varying intensity and frequency content. The experimental data (accelerations, pore pressures, displacements) will be analyzed using effective stress principles and compared to predictions from numerical tools like OpenSRA. The analysis will focus on correlating the severity of surface manifestations (e.g., settlement, lateral spreading) with the time-history of pore pressure ratio (r_u) and the state of inter-layer effective stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-quality, physics-based dataset on the full system response of stratified silts, directly informing and validating performance-based assessment frameworks like OpenSRA for a wider range of soil types. It will yield practical insights into the conditions under which silt layers contribute to severe ground failure versus acting as drainage barriers, improving the accuracy of seismic hazard maps and risk assessments for infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge testing of homogeneous silts exists, this study uniquely focuses on the system-scale interaction in stratified profiles, explicitly linking the measured severity of sediment ejecta and lateral deformations to the evolving effective stress state within a complex, layered deposit, a gap between element tests and field case histories.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Site-Response Divergence: A Performance-Based Assessment of Free-Field vs. Structure-Adjacent Ground Motions on Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic design and risk assessment platforms, like OpenSRA, often rely on free-field ground motion recordings or 1D site response analyses as input. For structures on potentially liquefiable soils, this practice assumes that free-field motions are fully representative of the shaking experienced by the foundation soil. However, the presence of a structure alters the stress state, drainage conditions, and kinematic constraints of the underlying soil, potentially leading to a divergence in seismic response that is not captured in free-field models, leading to unconservative or inefficient designs.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic ground response at a structure-foundation interface on liquefiable soil is systematically different from the free-field response at a 'similar' site, due to soil-structure interaction (SSI) effects that modify the soil's stress state and liquefaction triggering, leading to significant differences in key intensity measures and performance outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled effective stress analysis framework using finite difference or finite element codes (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC) validated against centrifuge test data. A parametric study will model a sliding, shallow-founded structure on a layered, liquefiable deposit, comparing the computed ground motions at the foundation level to those in an adjacent free-field column. The analysis will systematically vary structure properties (mass, footing geometry), soil state (relative density, permeability), and input motions. Key performance metrics, including foundation sliding displacement (linking to Bray's recent work), excess pore pressure ratios, and spectral accelerations, will be statistically compared between the two locations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanics-based framework to assess when free-field motions are insufficient for design, directly informing the ground motion selection and modification protocols used in tools like OpenSRA. It will deliver practical guidance, potentially in the form of modification factors or a decision flowchart, for engineers to account for this site-response divergence in performance-based assessment of infrastructure on liquefiable ground.
proposals.novelty: While SSI is studied for inertial loading, this proposal uniquely focuses on how the *presence* of a structure fundamentally alters the *free-field* site response mechanism itself for liquefiable soils, challenging a core assumption in regional seismic risk assessment. It integrates Bray's expertise in performance-based slope assessment and effective stress analysis into a novel SSI context.

proposals.title: A Data-Driven, Site-Specific Framework for Predicting Soil-to-Rock Spectral Acceleration Ratios Incorporating Liquefaction and Nonlinear Site Response
proposals.motivation: Current ground motion prediction equations (GMPEs) and site amplification factors, such as those in the NGA-West2 database, often rely on simplified site proxies like Vs30. For performance-based seismic assessment of critical infrastructure on liquefiable or nonlinear soils, these models can be insufficient, leading to inaccurate estimates of seismic demand. A refined, data-constrained relationship between soil and rock accelerations is essential for improving tools like OpenSRA and for more reliable risk assessments of slopes and buried infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating direct measures of soil state (e.g., relative density, fines content, and pre-shake pore pressure) and ground failure performance metrics (e.g., sliding displacement indices) into a statistical model will significantly improve the prediction of site-specific spectral acceleration ratios compared to models using Vs30 alone, particularly for sites that experienced liquefaction.
proposals.method: We will compile an expanded strong-motion dataset from global events (e.g., NGA-West2, K-NET, KiK-net), pairing soil station records with nearby rock reference stations. For each soil site, we will extract geotechnical parameters from available databases (e.g., CPT logs, SPT data) to characterize soil state and liquefaction resistance. Using a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework, we will develop predictive relationships for spectral acceleration ratios across periods. The model will treat nonlinear site response and liquefaction-induced degradation as latent variables informed by the geotechnical parameters and recorded ground motion intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, openly accessible predictive model for soil-to-rock acceleration ratios that directly integrates geotechnical site characterization, advancing beyond Vs30-based amplification. It will provide a more physically grounded input for performance-based seismic assessment of slope systems and infrastructure vulnerability within frameworks like OpenSRA, particularly for sites prone to liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of strong-motion data with detailed, pre-event soil state and resistance parameters to model amplification, moving from a purely geophysical correlation to a geotechnically informed predictive framework that accounts for the severity of soil nonlinearity and weakening.

proposals.title: A Geospatial Framework for Correlating Quaternary Geology with Seismic Damage Patterns: A Case Study of the 1994 Northridge Earthquake
proposals.motivation: Current seismic vulnerability assessments, including performance-based engineering and infrastructure risk platforms like OpenSRA, often rely on simplified site classifications (e.g., Vs30) that homogenize complex subsurface conditions. The 1994 Northridge earthquake produced highly localized damage, including liquefaction, lateral spreading, and ground failure, that existing models did not fully predict. A systematic, data-driven correlation between high-resolution geologic maps and observed damage is needed to move beyond broad proxies and improve predictive models for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: That spatially discrete damage patterns (e.g., liquefaction severity, landslide concentration, structural damage clusters) from the Northridge earthquake correlate strongly with specific Quaternary geologic units and their geomorphic context, and that these correlations can be quantified to create a predictive framework for localized seismic vulnerability.
proposals.method: We will develop a geospatial database integrating 1) high-resolution (1:24,000 scale) Quaternary geologic maps from the USGS, 2) compiled damage inventories from Northridge (liquefaction manifestations, landslide inventories, and structural damage surveys), and 3) terrain attributes (slope, curvature) from pre-2026 LiDAR DEMs. Using a Random Forest algorithm, we will perform supervised classification and regression to identify which geologic unit-terrain feature combinations are most predictive of observed damage intensity and type. The model will be validated via spatial cross-validation to assess its predictive power for unseen locations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a quantifiable, geologically-grounded correlation matrix linking specific subsurface conditions to damage modes, directly informing next-generation performance-based seismic assessment of slopes and liquefiable sites. The resulting framework will provide a methodology to refine regional-scale seismic risk models (like OpenSRA) by incorporating localized geologic vulnerability, leading to more targeted and cost-effective mitigation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While geology is acknowledged as important, this study moves beyond qualitative association to a quantitative, machine-learning-based correlation of detailed Quaternary units with a comprehensive damage dataset, creating a transferable predictive model for localized seismic effects that integrates directly with engineering vulnerability assessment.

proposals.title: Validation of 1D Effective-Stress Wave Propagation Models for Deep Alluvial Sites via Centrifuge Soil Column Experiments
proposals.motivation: Performance-based seismic assessment of critical infrastructure on deep, saturated alluvial deposits requires accurate prediction of site response, including pore pressure generation and liquefaction. Current 1D effective-stress analyses, such as those implemented in tools like OpenSRA, are widely used in practice but lack rigorous validation against high-quality physical data for deep soil columns under realistic stress conditions. This gap undermines confidence in risk assessments for natural gas pipelines and other distributed infrastructure crossing such geologic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Centrifuge model tests of instrumented, saturated sand columns subjected to base shaking will validate that contemporary 1D effective-stress wave propagation codes (e.g., using PM4Sand or UBC3D-PLM constitutive models) can accurately replicate the evolution of shear strains, excess pore pressures, and acceleration response spectra from deep within a soil profile to the surface.
proposals.method: We will design and conduct a series of dynamic centrifuge tests on uniform and layered soil columns, instrumented with accelerometers and pore pressure transducers at multiple depths. The tests will replicate prototype depths of ~30m at a scaling factor of N=50g. Input motions will represent a range of earthquake intensities. Numerical simulations will be performed using the 1D effective-stress site response algorithm in OpenSRA or a comparable finite-difference code, employing the PM4Sand constitutive model calibrated to the laboratory soil's state (relative density, confining pressure). The validation will focus on comparing the temporal and spectral evolution of recorded and computed accelerations and pore pressure ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark dataset and a clear validation framework for 1D effective-stress site response analysis, directly strengthening the physical basis for performance-based seismic assessment tools like OpenSRA. It will quantify the capabilities and limitations of these models for predicting key engineering demand parameters in deep alluvial sites, leading to more reliable liquefaction hazard evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge tests have validated liquefaction triggering models, a focused validation of full 1D wave propagation—including the coupled effects of stiffness degradation, dilation, and energy dissipation through depth in deep profiles—against high-quality centrifuge data for performance-based assessment purposes is a significant step beyond common element-test calibrations.

proposals.title: A Strain-Softening and Rate-Dependent Constitutive Framework for Performance-Based Seismic Displacement Analysis of Liquefiable Slopes
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessment of slopes, as implemented in tools like OpenSRA, often relies on rigid-plastic or simplified constitutive models. These models cannot capture the progressive strength loss during cyclic loading (strain-softening) or the dependence of soil resistance on loading rate, which are critical for predicting permanent displacements and post-liquefaction flow in sensitive soils. This limitation hinders the accurate estimation of seismic risk for infrastructure on or near slopes, particularly for natural gas pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a strain-softening, rate-dependent constitutive model within a coupled effective-stress finite element framework will yield significantly different and more accurate predictions of seismic slope displacement compared to analyses using a rigid-plastic assumption, especially for cases involving partial liquefaction and cyclic mobility.
proposals.method: The research will extend the effective stress analysis capabilities used in Bray's prior work on sediment ejecta. We will implement the PM4Sand constitutive model (version 3.1), a stress-ratio controlled, critical state-based bounding surface plasticity model with inherent strain-softening and modest rate-dependence, into the OpenSees finite element platform. A parametric study will be conducted on well-documented centrifuge or case history slopes, comparing displacement predictions from the new model against those from a traditional Newmark-type sliding block (rigid-plastic) analysis and recorded performance. Sensitivity analyses will isolate the contributions of strain-softening and rate-dependence to the final displacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, advanced constitutive modeling pathway for performance-based seismic slope assessment within the OpenSRA framework. It will deliver practical guidance on when simplified rigid-plastic analyses are unconservative or overly conservative for displacement prediction. The results will directly improve the fidelity of risk assessments for infrastructure interacting with liquefiable slopes.
proposals.novelty: While advanced constitutive models exist, their integration and systematic validation within a performance-based, infrastructure-focused seismic risk assessment workflow (like OpenSRA) for slope displacement is novel. The explicit quantification of the error introduced by the rigid-plastic assumption for modern, state-dependent soil models in this applied context has not been thoroughly explored.

proposals.title: Enhancing Performance-Based Seismic Assessment of Slopes and Infrastructure through Site-Specific Ground Motion Simulations
proposals.motivation: Current performance-based seismic assessments, such as those implemented in tools like OpenSRA, often rely on generic ground motion models (GMMs) or simplified scaling of recorded motions. These approaches fail to capture the complex, site-specific effects of wave propagation, basin geometry, and rupture directivity, which can significantly amplify or de-amplify shaking at critical infrastructure sites. For liquefaction and slope stability problems, where the seismic demand is highly sensitive to the frequency content and duration of shaking, this omission can lead to inaccurate estimates of deformation and risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing generic GMMs with physics-based, site-specific ground motion simulations will significantly reduce the epistemic uncertainty in seismic demand, leading to more accurate and reliable predictions of liquefaction-induced deformations and slope performance.
proposals.method: The research will integrate high-fidelity 3D ground motion simulations (e.g., using the SW4 or Hercules frameworks) into the existing OpenSRA and slope assessment workflows. For selected case-study sites (e.g., a liquefiable coastal plain and a steep, seismically active slope), we will simulate suites of motions for scenario earthquakes, explicitly modeling the 3D velocity structure and rupture kinematics. These simulated time series will then be used as input into coupled effective stress finite element analyses (using platforms like OpenSees or FLAC) to compute system performance metrics (e.g., sliding displacement, sediment ejecta volume). The results will be statistically compared against those derived from conventional GMM-based probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for incorporating advanced ground motion simulations into practical performance-based engineering. It will quantify the impact of site-specific wavefield effects on the assessed risk for liquefaction and slope stability, offering guidance for future seismic design and risk assessment protocols. The enhanced simulation capability will be made accessible within the open-source OpenSRA ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: While site-specific simulations are used in seismology, their systematic integration into performance-based geotechnical engineering workflows, particularly for effective stress analysis of liquefaction consequences and slope systems, represents a significant advancement beyond the current state-of-practice which is dominated by 1D site response and generic GMMs.

proposals.title: Calibration of a Performance-Based Effective Stress Procedure for Seismic Slope Stability Using Documented Case Histories of Flow and Lateral Spreading Failures
proposals.motivation: Performance-based seismic assessment of slopes, particularly those susceptible to liquefaction and cyclic softening, remains a critical challenge in geotechnical earthquake engineering. While advanced effective stress procedures exist, their predictive accuracy and reliability for estimating deformation or failure are often unproven against a comprehensive set of well-documented field case histories. This validation gap limits confidence in applying these methods for critical infrastructure risk assessments, such as those conducted with tools like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: A performance-based effective stress analysis procedure, integrating state-dependent constitutive models and the sliding-block method for deformation estimation, can be systematically calibrated to accurately back-analyze the observed performance (from stable to failed) of documented seismic slope failures.
proposals.method: The research will compile a database of 15-20 well-documented case histories of seismic slope failures involving liquefaction or cyclic softening, with known geometry, soil properties, and ground motion records. The procedure from Bray's prior work will be implemented, using the PM4Sand and PM4Silt constitutive models in finite difference software for coupled effective stress site response analysis. The resulting stress-strain-time histories will drive a sliding-block analysis (e.g., Newmark-type with weakening) to compute permanent displacements. A systematic calibration of key model parameters (e.g., post-cyclic residual strength, triggering criteria) will be conducted to match the observed field performance across all cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously calibrated and validated analytical framework for performance-based assessment of liquefiable slopes, directly increasing the reliability of seismic risk evaluations. It will yield a published database of calibrated case histories and recommended model parameters for practice, enhancing tools like OpenSRA for infrastructure risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, multi-case calibration of a full effective-stress-to-deformation procedure, moving beyond single-case studies or validation against centrifuge tests to a field-scale proof-of-concept, explicitly linking constitutive model performance to field-observed failure outcomes.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Framework for Seismic Hazard from Lateral Spreading: Integrating a Physics-Informed Displacement Model into PSHA
proposals.motivation: Current Probabilistic Seismic Hazard Analysis (PSHA) frameworks, which underpin modern building codes and risk assessments, primarily quantify ground shaking hazard. They do not directly output hazard curves for permanent ground displacement (PGD) from liquefaction-induced lateral spreading, a major cause of damage to buried pipelines, bridges, and port facilities. This gap limits the performance-based assessment of distributed infrastructure systems, a core challenge in projects like OpenSRA.
proposals.hypothesis: That a validated, effective-stress-based lateral spreading displacement model can be successfully embedded within a PSHA framework to produce site-specific hazard curves for PGD, and that this integrated approach will reveal that the probabilistic displacement hazard is governed by a different suite of earthquake scenarios than the peak ground acceleration hazard.
proposals.method: The research will extend the OpenSRA computational framework. We will implement the effective-stress-based sliding block displacement model from Bray's prior work as a modular 'ground failure' calculator. This module will be driven by intensity measures (e.g., Arias Intensity, PGA) from a logic tree of ground motion models within a standard PSHA engine (e.g., based on the USGS NSHMP framework). The analysis will propagate uncertainties from seismic source characterization, ground motion prediction, and the displacement model itself via Monte Carlo simulation. The final output will be hazard curves for PGD at specified return periods, disaggregated to identify controlling earthquake magnitudes and distances.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source, fully probabilistic methodology for calculating lateral spreading displacement hazard, directly usable in performance-based engineering for lifelines and slopes. It will provide a rigorous, transparent alternative to deterministic or simplified empirical approaches, enabling risk-informed decision-making for critical infrastructure. The results will advance the state of practice in seismic risk assessment for liquefiable sites.
proposals.novelty: While PSHA for ground shaking is mature, and empirical lateral spreading models exist, the novel integration of a physics-informed displacement model into a full PSHA logic tree to produce probabilistic displacement hazard is a significant methodological advance. It moves beyond correlating displacement with PGA to a rigorous integration over all potential seismic sources and ground motions.

ideas.title: Remote Sensing-Constrained Seismic Source Inversion via Liquefaction Manifestation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes rapid, wide-area remote sensing of liquefaction-induced ground deformation (from InSAR and LiDAR) with inverse analysis techniques to estimate the ground motions that caused the damage. The spatially continuous maps of settlement and tilt provide a massive, objective constraint for inverting key shaking parameters, such as peak ground velocity and significant duration, across the affected region. This approach bypasses the need for dense instrumental networks, creating a novel method to rapidly characterize seismic source complexity and shaking distribution following major earthquakes, especially in areas with poor seismic station coverage. The resulting refined shaking maps can then improve regional loss assessments and guide urgent response efforts.

ideas.title: Disentangling Fabric from Confounders: A Causal Framework for Fill Placement, Liquefaction, and Damage
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to establish causal links between reclamation methods and seismic performance. It employs a three-pillar approach: 1) rigorous laboratory characterization of fill materials to define intrinsic properties independent of placement, 2) high-fidelity numerical modeling that synthetically constructs fills with method-specific fabric (density, stratification, anisotropy) within otherwise identical virtual prototypes to isolate fabric's causal role, and 3) physical validation via geotechnical centrifuge testing on carefully prepared models that replicate these construction-specific fabrics. The outcome is a predictive, mechanics-based framework that quantifies how placement-induced fabric directly dictates damage patterns, controlling for the confounding factors that plague field observations.

ideas.title: Autonomous soil resilience: designing self-adapting geomaterials for seismic sequences
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static soil design to create geomaterials capable of real-time adaptation during an earthquake sequence. The core idea is to engineer soil compositions with embedded, dormant agents that are activated by the mechanical and hydrological conditions of initial strong shaking. These agents then trigger rapid, beneficial changes—such as pore-filling precipitation or particle rearrangement—that strengthen the soil matrix in the critical period between seismic events. The goal is to produce soils whose post-shaking state is more resistant to subsequent liquefaction than their initial state, effectively 'healing' or 'hardening' in response to the first tremor.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Imaging of Shallow, Heterogeneous Soils to Decouple Liquefaction from Deposit Uniformity
ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced medical imaging (e.g., micro-CT, MRI) to systematically investigate the internal structure of shallow, heterogeneous, or native soil deposits that are conventionally considered low-liquefaction risk. By creating high-resolution 3D models of pore networks, particle contacts, and fabric anisotropy in such soils, the research aims to identify specific microscale configurations—such as localized loose pockets, abrupt permeability boundaries, or delicate skeletal structures—that could predispose even thin or variable strata to severe liquefaction. This allows for a physics-based challenge to empirical rules, moving hazard prediction from deposit-scale generalizations to a mechanistic understanding rooted in quantifiable soil architecture.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Regional Liquefaction Forecasting
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the problem of scaling up liquefaction hazard forecasting. In CFD, AMR efficiently solves complex flows by using a coarse grid everywhere and refining it only in critical regions. The adapted idea is to develop probabilistic liquefaction models for entire cities that begin with a coarse-scale assessment based on regional geology and hydrology. This coarse model then guides the dynamic, automated refinement of the spatial analysis grid specifically in high-probability or high-uncertainty hazard zones, such as vulnerable soil deposits under expected seismic shaking. This creates a dual-scale, computationally efficient forecasting system that provides high-resolution detail where it matters most for risk assessment, while maintaining a tractable regional-scale model.

ideas.title: Dynamic, High-Resolution Probabilistic Liquefaction Forecasting with Real-Time Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-scale, data-assimilative forecasting framework for liquefaction hazard. It integrates a regional probabilistic model with a high-resolution geospatial mapping engine to produce city-wide forecasts. The core innovation is the system's ability to ingest real-time data streams—such as ground motion recordings, pore-pressure sensor data, and updated water table measurements—as an earthquake unfolds. This allows the model to dynamically update and downscale its probabilistic forecasts, transforming static pre-event hazard maps into live, high-resolution severity assessments that evolve with the seismic event, providing critical guidance for emergency managers and infrastructure operators.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Soil Network Tomography for Infrastructure Resilience
ideas.description: This research direction fuses network theory with advanced medical imaging to create a novel framework for infrastructure resilience analysis. It uses techniques like MRI or CT scanning to generate high-resolution, three-dimensional maps of subsurface soil variability, pore structure, and material interfaces. These detailed soil models are then used to define the constitutive properties and connection strengths between infrastructure nodes (e.g., building foundations, buried pipes) within a physics-based interaction network. This allows for the simulation and analysis of failure propagation—such as from liquefaction or differential settlement—through the actual, imaged soil medium, moving beyond abstract network models to grounded, mechanistic predictions of systemic risk.

ideas.title: Designing Soils with a 'Metabolic' Response to Sequential Seismic Loading
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of sequential, triggered release from biomedical drug delivery systems to geotechnical engineering. Instead of analyzing soil failure, we proactively engineer soil compositions to withstand multiple liquefaction-triggering events. The analogous solution from biomedical engineering involves designing materials with components that activate at distinct thresholds, like multi-layered drug delivery capsules. Adapted to this context, the research would involve designing composite soils where specific granular components, engineered inclusions (such as biodegradable polymers or crushable mineral shells), or chemical agents are designed to activate in a sequence. The first seismic event would trigger a beneficial, strength-preserving response from the first 'layer' (e.g., releasing a pore-fluid viscosifier or inducing controlled dilation), which modifies the soil's state to resist liquefaction more effectively during the following aftershock or mainshock, thereby creating a soil with a built-in, multi-stage defense mechanism.

ideas.title: Adapting Polymer Chain Scission Models for Sequential Soil Fabric Degradation
ideas.description: This idea applies the 'chain scission' degradation model from polymer chemistry to the problem of sequential soil liquefaction. In polymers, kinetic models track the cumulative breaking of molecular chains under cyclic stress to predict loss of integrity. Analogously, we propose to model soil fabric as a network of load-bearing inter-particle contacts (akin to polymer chains). Each seismic loading cycle can break or weaken a certain number of these contacts—a process termed 'fabric scission.' By adapting the kinetic equations from polymer science, we can develop a state variable that quantifies cumulative fabric damage across multiple earthquakes. This variable would function within a constitutive model to predict the accelerated degradation of shear strength and increased liquefaction susceptibility in subsequent seismic events, moving beyond models calibrated for single events.

ideas.title: Multi-Fidelity Liquefaction Forecasting: From Regional Screening to Site-Specific Digital Twins
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid forecasting system that overcomes the resolution gap in regional models. It employs a regional probabilistic model for initial hazard screening, which then guides and initializes high-resolution, site-specific digital twins for critical zones. These digital twins assimilate local geological, hydrological, and geotechnical data to provide asset-scale probabilistic forecasts of liquefaction severity and consequences. The system is designed for progressive refinement, allowing planners to prioritize investigation resources and engineers to obtain actionable, location-specific risk assessments.

ideas.title: A Multi-Sensor, Machine Learning Framework for Probabilistic Liquefaction Susceptibility Mapping
ideas.description: This research adapts the data fusion and predictive modeling approach from precision agriculture to the problem of mapping liquefaction severity. In precision agriculture, solutions integrate drone/satellite imagery, ground sensors, and weather data with machine learning to create detailed, predictive maps of crop yield variability. The analogous solution is adapted by treating liquefaction susceptibility as the target variable. The proposed research will create high-resolution, probabilistic liquefaction severity maps by developing a machine learning framework that correlates and fuses multi-source geospatial data: InSAR data for subtle ground deformation precursors, ERT and CPT logs for 3D soil composition, LiDAR-derived microtopography to infer water table depth variability, and shaking intensity from seismic networks. This integrated model will predict not just binary susceptibility but continuous severity grades with associated spatial uncertainty, providing a much more detailed and actionable tool for seismic risk assessment.

ideas.title: A coupled network-continuum framework for simulating physics-based infrastructure failure propagation through soil
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid computational framework that couples a topological network model of buildings and lifelines with a high-resolution, physics-based simulation of the surrounding soil continuum. The network provides the system-of-systems connectivity, while the continuum model explicitly simulates the geomechanical response, including stress redistribution, pore pressure changes, and progressive soil failure. This coupling enables the physically realistic simulation of cascading failures, where the collapse or excessive settlement of one structure alters the soil's load-bearing capacity for its neighbors. The approach moves beyond abstract propagation graphs to model the actual mechanistic drivers of infrastructure interdependence during seismic or climatic hazards.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Attribute Analysis from Geophysics for High-Resolution Soil Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from petroleum geophysics, where seismic attribute analysis is used to extract detailed structural and stratigraphic features from subsurface reflection data. The analogous problem is visualizing fine-scale heterogeneity within a 3D volume where the features of interest (e.g., pore networks, soil aggregates) are complex and not fully described by raw intensity values. The adapted research idea applies this principle to soil science by using CT or MRI scans of soil cores as the base 3D data volume. A computational pipeline would be developed to calculate a suite of novel 'soil imaging attributes'—mathematical transforms of the voxel data—to highlight and quantify specific soil architectural components. This would move beyond simple density/threshold-based segmentation to automatically identify features like connected pore pathways, soil aggregate interfaces, root remnants, and textural anomalies, providing a powerful, automated tool for quantifying soil microstructure and its impact on hydraulic and biological processes.

ideas.title: Seismic Shaking as a Site-Specific Resonance Problem: A Geophysical Signal Processing Approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that proximity to a fault is the primary control on shaking intensity by introducing a sophisticated solution adapted from audio engineering. In acoustics, room equalization (EQ) is used to compensate for a room's resonant properties to achieve a flat frequency response, regardless of listener position or source power. Analogously, this research proposes to treat local geology as a resonant filter that processes seismic waves. The adapted solution involves developing geophysical signal processing techniques to deconvolve the site-specific transfer function from recorded ground motions. This empirically derived 'site filter' would then be used to predict shaking intensity for future seismic events by convolving the filter with simulated or recorded source functions, fundamentally shifting the paradigm from distance-based attenuation models to site-specific waveform prediction based on calibrated local resonance.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological SIR Models to Analyze Cascading Infrastructure Failures in Shared Soil
ideas.description: This research adapts the epidemiological Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) model to analyze failure propagation in civil infrastructure networks sharing a soil medium. In the analogy, the SIR model's network of individuals (nodes) and disease transmission via contacts parallels a network of buildings and lifelines (nodes) where 'failure' propagates through shared geotechnical conditions (soil). The adapted solution involves defining infrastructure nodes with states (e.g., Intact, Failed, Recovered/Retrofitted), where transition probabilities from Intact to Failed are a function of geotechnical 'contact' (e.g., soil stress redistribution, pore pressure changes) from nearby failed nodes and the node's inherent geotechnical vulnerability. This allows simulation of cascading failure scenarios to identify which nodes, if reinforced or 'immunized,' would best contain the cascade, providing a quantitative tool for resilience-based infrastructure planning and retrofit prioritization.

ideas.title: Hybrid Sensor Fusion for Immediate Post-Event Structural Triage
ideas.description: This system integrates a pre-deployed network of low-cost, connected ground sensors (MEMS tiltmeters and GNSS) on critical infrastructure with traditional remote sensing. Following an event, the ground network provides sub-hourly, weather-independent data on settlement and tilt for instrumented sites, enabling immediate triage. These ground-truth points then guide the tasking of satellite InSAR and airborne LiDAR to efficiently map differential movement across the broader area, overcoming cloud cover and data latency issues. The fusion creates a rapid, validated damage assessment picture within hours, not days.

ideas.title: A Scalable Hybrid Sensing and ML Framework for Inferring 3D Soil Architecture
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the scalability barrier of medical imaging by combining targeted, portable low-field NMR sensing with machine learning. The system collects rapid, in-situ NMR readings at strategic field locations to capture fine-scale soil-water interactions and organic composition. These point measurements serve as ground truth to train a convolutional neural network that learns to translate widely available, field-scale geophysical and remote sensing data into detailed 3D models of soil pore structure and heterogeneity. The result is a practical, scalable method for generating medical-imaging-quality subsurface insights without the prohibitive cost and immobility of clinical scanners.
